segments.s652-g97: همه شهر ازو پرگنهکار شد is a simple past/result clause: همه شهر (the entire city) is the subject phrase, ازو ('because of him' / 'from him') gives the cause, پرگنهکار is a predicative adjective 'sinful / filled with sin', and شد is the past-tense verb 'became'. Word order is Subject–(adverbial/cause)–Predicate–Verb, typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s652-g98: سر بخت‌برگشته بیدار شد: سر here names a person (poetic use 'head' → 'the man/person'), بخت‌برگشته is a compound adjective 'ill‑fated / whose fortune has turned', بیدار is the predicate adjective 'awake', and شد is the past verb 'became / awoke'. The whole phrase means 'the ill‑fated man awoke' with the adjectival phrase following the noun and the copula شد marking the past result.
segments.s652-g99: خبر زین به شهر مداین رسید is a canonical Persian clause: خبر 'news' is the topic, زین here is an archaic/genitive marker 'of this' (not the literal 'saddle'), به + شهر مداین gives the goal 'to the city Madā'in', and رسید is the past verb 'reached / was received'. Word order places the news/topic first, then the destination, then the verb.
segments.s652-g100: ازان که آمد از پور کسری پدید is a subordinate content clause introduced by ازان که 'from that/that', functioning like 'that' in English. آمد is simple past 'came/arrived', از پور کسری reads 'from the son/descendant of Kisra' (پور = son), and پدید is a literary infinitive/past participle meaning 'appeared / came into view'. The clause reports what the news contained.
segments.s652-g101: نگهبان مرز مداین ز راه is a noun phrase with modifiers: نگهبان 'the guard' modified by مرزِ مداین 'of the frontier of Madā'in'; ز راه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'by the road / from the road' giving route or means. Classical Persian often uses ز = 'from/by' instead of modern از.
segments.s652-g102: سواری برافگند نزدیک شاه contains the verb phrase: سواری 'a rider / horseman' is the direct object/agent being sent, برافگند is an archaic perfective 'threw (forth) / let fly' used here causatively to mean 'sent/dispatch(ed)', and نزدیک شاه 'toward the king / near the king' gives the destination. In older poetic diction برافگند serves where modern Persian would use فرستاد.
segments.s652-g103: سخن هرچ بشنید با او بگفت is a clause with object and report verb: سخن 'the words/news' and هرچ 'whatever / all that' form the object (whatever he had heard), بشنید is past 'he heard', با او 'to him / with him' marks the indirect object, and بگفت is the reporting verb 'he said / told'. This is the reportage construction 'he told him whatever he had heard.'
segments.s652-g104: چنین آگهی کی بود در نهفت is a quoted/questioning clause: چنین 'such / so', آگهی 'news/information', کی is interrogative 'who' (classical usage) asking about an agent, بود is past 'was', در نهفت is a compound meaning 'kept/hidden inside' (در = in, نهفت = hidden). Together it reads rhetorically 'Who had kept such news hidden?'.
segments.s652-g41: جهانی (subject) + پر آشوب (adjectival phrase) + گردد (verb) + کنون (temporal adverb). The word order is typical Persian: noun phrase 'جهانی پر آشوب' ('a world full of turmoil') followed by the verb 'گردد' (here ‘becomes’), and the sentence-final time adverb 'کنون' ('now') places the action in the present. 'گردد' is an older/poetic form of گشتن/شدن meaning 'become'.
segments.s652-g42: Literally 'they lead/bring every direction by bad guidance.' بیارند is a classical/poetic verbal form meaning 'they bring/they lead' (third-person plural); 'هر سو' is an adverbial phrase 'in every direction / everywhere'; 'به' introduces the manner/instrument ('by, with'); and 'بد رهنمون' is a compound noun phrase 'bad guidance' serving as the instrument of leading. The clause describes people being guided badly in all directions.
segments.s652-g43: A compact nominal phrase 'جهاندار بیدار کسری' ('the alert/world-ruler Khosrow') followed by بمرد. In epic Persian بمرد here functions as the narrative past 'died' (an older/poetic form of مردن). The epithets precede the proper name in typical Persian word order.
segments.s652-g44: 'زمان و زمین' is a coordinated direct object meaning 'time and the land/realm'; 'دیگری را' marks 'another' as the definite object of transfer; and 'سپرد' is the past-tense verb 'entrusted/handed over.' Word order is Subject (understood from previous clause) → Object → Verb, with را marking the object.
segments.s652-g45: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'because of/from', introducing the cause 'مرگ پدر' ('the father's death'); 'شاد شد' is a two-word verb phrase 'became happy/rejoiced' (شاد = happy, شد = became); and 'نوش‌زاد' is the subject appearing after the verb for emphasis ('the newborn'). This is a standard causal construction with the experiencer as subject.
segments.s652-g46: This is a jussive/wish clause introduced by که: 'که هرگز ورا نام نوشین مباد' = 'may the name Nushin never be his.' هرگز 'never' negates the wish; ورا is the indirect object 'to him'; نام 'name'; نوشین here functions as an epithet/name 'pleasant' but used nominally; and مباد is an archaic optative/negative copula meaning 'may it not be.'
segments.s652-g47: 'برین' is a prepositional phrase 'on this / to this' (بر + این); 'داستان' is the noun 'tale/story'; 'زد' here is a classical poetic use of زدن meaning 'uttered / spoke / narrated' rather than literal 'to strike'; and 'یکی مرد پیر' is 'a certain old man' (یکی indicating indefiniteness). The verb precedes the nominal phrase for rhetorical emphasis in epic style.
segments.s652-g48: که‌گر is a fused conditional 'که گر' = 'if/ whoever'; شادی از مرگ is 'joy because of death' (شادی = joy; از مرگ = at/about death); هرگز ممیر is a negative jussive/imperative 'never die' used here as the old man's admonition/blessing. The clause reads as a conditional admonition: 'If you rejoice at death, may you never die.'
segments.s652-g25: This clause says who changed religious allegiance. ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source 'دین پدر' = 'the father's religion' (genitive); then 'کیش مادر' = 'the mother's sect/creed' is the object taken. گرفت is a simple past 'took/assumed'. Word order is source → new possession → verb (poetic).
segments.s652-g26: زمانه is 'the age/the times' as subject; بدو is the short form of 'به او' ('toward him' / 'at him'); مانده is perfect/past participle meaning 'remained' or 'was left' and here functions like 'stood/was left'; اندر شگفت means 'in wonder/astonishment' (شگفت as noun). The clause means 'the times remained/stood amazed at him.'
segments.s652-g27: چنان ... گشته is the result clause's first half: چنان = 'so/thus', تنگدل گشته = 'has become narrow‑hearted / melancholy' (گشته = become). زو here is the contracted poetic form of 'از او' = 'because of him/on his account'. شهریار is the subject 'the king'.
segments.s652-g28: که introduces the result clause ('so that/that'); از گل نیامد = 'nothing came from the flower/rose' (نیامد = did not come). جز = 'except'; از خار بار = literally 'a load from thorns' — بار here is 'load/pack'. The whole line is a poetic image: no blossom produced but a burden of thorns.
segments.s652-g29: در = 'in' or 'within'; کاخ = 'palace'; و = 'and'; فرخنده modifies ایوان (an epithet meaning 'auspicious/fortunate' or 'splendid'); ایوان = 'portico/porch'; او = 'his' (possessive pronoun referring back to the person mentioned). The phrase locates what was closed.
segments.s652-g30: ببستند is past 'they shut/closed' (third person plural); و = 'and'; کردند here means 'they made/caused' (i.e., they made into); زندان = 'prison'; او = 'his' (the one belonging to him). Together: 'they shut ... and made it his prison.'
segments.s652-g31: نشستنگهش is a compound meaning 'his seat/place of sitting' (نشستنگه + enclitic ش = 'his/its seat'). جند means 'army/garrison' and شاپور is the name 'Shapur'; بود is the past copula 'was'. So the clause states where his seat/garrison was located.
segments.s652-g32: This gives location/distance: از ایران = 'from Iran'; وز here is the poetic contraction 'و ز' = 'and from' (an archaic fused form); باختر = 'the West' or 'west'; دور بود = 'was far'. The whole clause: 'it was far from Iran and the West.' Note: وز is an old/poetic connective (not the literal senses listed in the lemma table).
segments.s652-g49: This relative clause uses کو as a poetic relative/marker ('who/that') and places the verb at the end (SOV-like order): پسر (subject) + کو (relative) + ز راه پدر (prepositional phrase 'from/away from father's way') + بگذرد (present/subjunctive form 'passes/departs'). بگذرد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive used generically ('who departs').
segments.s652-g50: خوانیمش is the verb خوانیم + enclitic object -ش (him/it): 'we call/read him so.' ستم‌کاره is the predicate noun 'oppressor/tyrant.' ار is an archaic short form of اگر ('if'); بی‌خرد is a predicate adjective 'foolish/unwise.' The clause means 'we call him an oppressor if (he is) foolish.'
segments.s652-g51: اگر introduces a conditional. بیخ حنظل 'the root of colocynth' is the subject phrase; بود‌تر is a comparative/adverbial 'even more / still more' modifying خشک 'dry/barren.' The adjective phrase follows the noun, typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s652-g52: نشاید is an impersonal/archaic negative of شایستن: 'it would not be fitting/should not be.' که introduces the content clause ('that...'). بار آورد شاخ مشک literally 'a branch should bring/bear a load of musk' — آورد is present tense 'bring/carry' used here in a hortatory/subjunctive sense after نشاید.
segments.s652-g53: چرا here is rhetorical ('wherefore/indeed'); گشت باید is an inverted order meaning 'it must become / it must be so' (گشت = become, باید = must). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed'/'so'), and زان سرشت 'from that nature' gives the reason — this is a causal/naturally resulting statement.
segments.s652-g54: که introduces the explanatory clause: پالیزبانش is پالیزبان (gardener) + -ش (its/his), 'its gardener' or 'the gardener of it.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and بکشت is an archaic past/preterite form meaning 'planted / made' (here 'planted it'). The clause states the gardener planted it from the beginning.
segments.s652-g55: اگر introduces a conditional. میل یابد is a verbal phrase 'finds an inclination / becomes inclined' (یابد = 'finds/attains'); همی is emphatic. سوی خاک is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the earth/soil.' The verb phrase describes a tendency or inclination.
segments.s652-g56: ببرد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive meaning 'it will carry/take (away)' or 'be carried'; here it functions as the result clause's verb. The list introduced by ز (from) names causes/agents: خورشید (sun), وز (poetic conjunction/particle joining items), باد (wind), و (and), خاک (dust/earth). The series names forces that carry it away or act upon it.
segments.s652-g129: وزان is a compound/poetic connector meaning “and from that / and from those” (و + زان) and introduces the group; پرگناهان is an adjective phrase ‘full of sins / sinful’ modifying زندان‌شکن; زندان‌شکن is a compound noun ‘jail‑breaker / liberator’. The whole noun phrase names the group under discussion.
segments.s652-g130: که begins a relative clause ‘who/that’; گشتند is the past plural of گشتن meaning ‘they became / they turned (into)’; با + نوش‌زاد انجمن is a prepositional phrase ‘with a newborn/freshly‑formed assembly’, where نوش‌زاد (newborn/fresh‑born) adjectivally modifies انجمن (assembly). The clause therefore modifies زندان‌شکن.
segments.s652-g131: چنین روز is a fronted temporal phrase ‘on such a day’; اگر is the conditional marker ‘if’; چشم دارد کسی literally ‘has an eye someone’ = ‘someone has sight / someone sees’. The verb دارد appears before the subject کسی, a common order in Persian for emphasis.
segments.s652-g132: سزد‌گر is an agentive/elliptical conditional expression derived from سزد (to befit) + گر, here functioning like ‘if it is fitting / if one deems it fit’; نماند is the present subjunctive/optative sense ‘should not remain / not remain’; به گیتی modifies نماند (‘in the world’); بسی modifies the extent ‘long / much / for many (days)’. The clause therefore expresses a wish/advice conditional: if it befit, one should not remain long in the world.
segments.s652-g133: که here introduces an explanatory clause ‘for / that’; جز مرگ را is fronted for emphasis ‘except death’ (را marks the definite object/emphasis); کس ز مادر نزاد is a relative/basic clause ‘anyone born of a mother’ where نزاد is a poetic past form meaning ‘was born / came into being’. Together the phrase says no one born of a mother is excepted from death.
segments.s652-g134: ز کسری بر آغاز تا نوش‌زاد marks the span ‘from Kisra at the beginning to the newborn’: ز = ‘from’, کسری = the royal name Kisra (here ‘Khosrow’), بر آغاز = ‘at/on the outset/beginning’ (بر used poetically), and تا = ‘until/to’ introducing the other end of the span (نوش‌زاد = ‘the newborn’). The whole phrase specifies the inclusive range that none escape.
segments.s652-g135: رها نیست is a fixed negated predicate ‘is not free / is not released’; از introduces the source or cause ‘from / of’; چنگ و منقار مرگ = ‘the claw and the beak of Death’ where مرگ follows to indicate possession/agent (‘of Death’). Word order is poetic but straightforward: nothing is free from Death’s claws and beak.
segments.s652-g136: پی here means ‘after / in pursuit of’; it links a list of small and large creatures: پشه و مور ‘the gnat/mosquito and ant’ and با پیل و کرگ ‘with the elephant and (a) bird’ (با joins the pair). The construction shows equal application: death pursues both the tiniest and the greatest.
segments.s652-g89: بسی is an adverbial quantifier 'many/much' (here indicating numerousness) modifying انجمن. انجمن کرد is a light noun+verb collocation 'made/held an assembly' where کرد is the past tense 'did/made'. بر خویشتن means 'to/for himself' (literally 'on himself') and indicates that the gathering was to his side or under his command; the phrase links forward to name who was gathered.
segments.s652-g90: This is a noun phrase describing those gathered: سواران 'horsemen' (plural) followed by two postnominal modifiers coordinated by و. گردنکش is an adjective/epithet 'rebellious/headstrong' and تیغ‌زن is an agent noun 'sword‑wielder/swordsman'; the modifiers come after the head noun in typical Persian word order.
segments.s652-g91: فراز is an adverb 'up/on high' placed before the verb for emphasis. آمدندش is the past plural verb آمدند 'they came' with the enclitic -ش 'to him/at him' (object/indirect object). تنی سی‌هزار is a numeral phrase: تنی ('a certain/in bodily') + سی‌هزار 'thirty thousand' specifying the number/persons — together indicating 'thirty thousand in person'.
segments.s652-g92: همه 'all/everyone' quantifies the following role nouns. نیزه‌داران is a compound noun 'spear‑bearers' (lancers/spearmen) and خنجرگزار 'dagger‑bearers' describes another class of armed men; this phrase further characterizes the thirty thousand mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s652-g93: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'. نامه بنوشت is a past action 'wrote a letter' — بنوشت is a poetic/older past form of 'نوشت' (to write) with کرد/ب- formation; the verb takes its object after it. نزدیک خویش here means 'to one near himself' or 'to his close associate(s)' (نزدیک used as a noun/adjective + خویش the possessive), specifying the addressee of the letter; the clause sets up the action and its immediate recipient.
segments.s652-g94: ز قیصر is the source attribution 'from Caesar' (ز is the poetic 'from'). چو introduces a comparison/simile 'as/like', leading into آیین تاریک خویش 'his dark/secret practice' where آیین 'custom/practice' is modified by تاریک 'dark' and completed by the possessive خویش (here 'his'). The whole segment explains the letter's purported origin and the manner in which it was written.
segments.s652-g95: که here is the clause‑introducing 'that/which'. بر جندشاپور is a locative 'over/above Gundeshapur' (بر + place). مهتر 'chief/leader' is the predicate nominal and تویی is the archaic/poetic second‑person present 'thou art' (copula comes after the predicate in this construction), so the clause states 'that you are the chief over Gundeshapur.'
segments.s652-g96: Two coordinated epithets precede the possessor: هم‌آواز 'like‑minded/ally' and هم‌کیش 'coreligionist/fellow‑believer' joined by و. قیصر follows as the possessor ('of Caesar'), and the clause closes with تویی 'thou art' — altogether: 'you are Caesar's ally and fellow‑believer.'
segments.s652-g105: This is a noun phrase where فرستاده (the messenger) is followed by برسان, here functioning as a participial/verbal modifier meaning 'having brought' (classical/epic usage) rather than an imperative. آب روان is a compound noun meaning 'running/flowing water' with آب 'water' modified by روان 'flowing'. The whole segment = 'the messenger who brought running water'.
segments.s652-g106: بیامد is the past narrative 'came'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک) indicating approach, and نوشین‌روان is an epithet/name 'the sweet‑souled one' placed after the preposition. Together: 'came near the sweet‑souled one.'
segments.s652-g107: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said/related'. آنچ (آنچه) is a relative/object phrase 'that which/what'. بشنید is past 'he heard', so آنچ بشنید = 'what (he) had heard'. و links the clauses, and نامه بداد is 'he gave the letter' where نامه is 'letter' but بداد as listed in the provided senses does not match the verbal meaning 'gave' here (classical past of دادن), so the supplied sense list is inadequate.
segments.s652-g108: سخنها 'the words/speeches' is the subject; که introduces a relative/clausal link 'that/which'. پیدا شد is a light-passive/auxiliary construction 'became known/appeared' (پیدا here = 'found/appeared', شد as auxiliary 'became/was'), and از نوش‌زاد indicates origin/source 'from Nush‑zād' (an epithet or name). Overall: 'the words that came from Nush‑zād became revealed.'
segments.s652-g109: ازو is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from it/of it' placed before the subject for emphasis. شاه is the subject 'the king'. بشنید is past 'heard (of)'. و links the clauses, and نامه بخواند is past 'he read the letter' (بخواند here = read/recited). The subject ('the king') carries both verbs.
segments.s652-g110: غمی گشت is a common Persian idiom 'sorrow became' = 'sorrow arose' (غمی = grief; گشت = became). زان کار = 'from that affair/for that reason' (زان = from that), and و تیره بماند links with 'and remained dark/gloomy'—تیره an adjective 'gloomy/ominous' and بماند (narrative past here) 'remained'. The clause describes the emotional consequence.
segments.s652-g111: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. با introduces the accompaniment 'with', and موبد is the title/name of the priest; سرفراز is a following epithet 'proud/honorable' modifying موبد. The whole phrase = 'the sovereign with the honorable mōbed.'
segments.s652-g112: نشست is the past narrative 'sat' or 'took a seat/held a session'; و links to the next clause. سخن رفت is an idiomatic intransitive use meaning 'talk/conversation went on' (literally 'words went'), رفت here = 'proceeded/was spent'. چندی 'for a while' is an adverbial phrase, and به راز 'in secret/privately' modifies how they spoke.
segments.s652-g169: ازین دست is a prepositional noun phrase meaning “from this kind” (از + این written as ین here); it modifies سخن. خوارست is the predicate 'خوار است' contracted to one word meaning 'is despicable/lowly' and applies to سخن. بر ما places the direction/target of that speech ('against us'). Word order here is topical: prepositional phrase → predicate → target (speech is despicable from such people, directed at us).
segments.s652-g170: ز کردار ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'because of / on account of their deeds' (ز = because of/of). تو دل بد مکن is a 2nd-person negative imperative: مکن = “do not (do)”; دل بد (literally 'make the heart bad') means 'become upset/angry' or 'let it trouble your heart'. The subject 'تو' is explicit for emphasis.
segments.s652-g171: مرا here marks the experiencer ('to me' / 'I have') and the clause is existential: 'بیم و باک' ('fear and dread') are the nouns. از + جهانداورست means 'of the World‑Judge' (جهانداور + ست where -ست is the copula 'is' built into the compound). The whole phrase reads roughly 'To me there is fear and dread of the world's Judge.'
segments.s652-g172: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for' / 'because'): 'که از دانش برتران برترست' = 'for he is superior to the foremost among the learned.' از دانش marks the domain ('of knowledge' / 'among the learned'), برتران = 'the superior/foremost ones' and برترست is the predicate 'is superior.'
segments.s652-g173: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'; that is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. شد is the verb 'become' (here used as part of the subordinate clause); جان ما is the subject ('our life / our soul'); بی‌سپاس is a predicate adjective 'ungrateful.' The line means 'It must not be that our life become ungrateful.'
segments.s652-g174: به نزدیک = 'in the presence of / near to.' یزدان is 'God' and نیکی‑شناس is an epithet 'knower of good' or 'recognizer of good' modifying God. Together they form the locative/benefactive phrase 'before the God who recognizes goodness.'
segments.s652-g175: مرا داد = 'he gave to me' (مرا = 'to me' as object/experiencer; داد = 'gave'). پیروزی و فرهی are the direct objects joined by و: 'victory and nobility/honor' (فرهی here is an epithet meaning noble/honorable). The canonical order is indirect object (مرا) + verb + direct objects.
segments.s652-g176: فزونی و ... شاهنشهی continues the list of gifts: فزونی = 'increase/abundance' and دیهیم modifies شاهنشهی (an epithet or qualifier attached to 'kingship' / 'imperial rule'). Together they mean 'increase and [qualifying adjective] imperial kingship' — i.e. growth/abundance and the (augmented) royal dominion. In poetry such compounds and epithets are placed after one another for rhythm.
segments.s652-g9: A string of coordinated adjectival descriptions modifying the person: خردمند 'wise' and هشیار 'alert'. The phrase با رای uses با + a noun (رای) to mean 'with (good) counsel' or 'of sound judgment'; شرم here means 'modesty'. The conjunction و simply links the adjectives in the normal Persian order.
segments.s652-g10: سخن گفتنش is a nominalized clause: سخن 'speech' + گفتنش (گفتن + ـش) = 'her/his speaking' or 'the way she/he spoke' used as a noun phrase. خوب is a predicative adjective modifying that noun; و links the second predicate آوای نرم, where آوای (the genitive/construct form of آواز) + نرم describes 'a gentle/soft voice'.
segments.s652-g11: برین سان is an archaic/poetic formula meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این + سان fused). شاه is the subject and داشت is the past tense verb 'had'; زنی is the direct object 'a woman'. پرمایه is an adjective after داشت describing that woman ('of abundant substance/worth'). Word order is characteristic of classical Persian where adjectives or modifiers may appear postpositively.
segments.s652-g12: Two similes introduced by repeated به: 'به بالای سرو' = 'to/upon the height of a cypress' (i.e. as tall as a cypress) and 'به دیدار ماه' = 'to/like the sight/appearance of the moon' (i.e. as lovely as the moon). In classical Persian به + noun can introduce a likeness; بالای is 'the top/height of' and دیدار is used poetically as 'sight/meeting' (here 'sight' of the moon).
segments.s652-g13: This is a compact, exclamatory line. بدین (here read as 'thus' or 'so') opens the clause; مسیحا is a vocative interjection 'O Christ!' used as an exclamation. بد این ماه‌روی is an exclamatory appraisal 'woe/bad is this moon-faced one' (بد = 'bad/woe', این ماه‌روی = 'this moon‑faced [beauty]'). Poetic inversion and abrupt vocative are typical of epic diction.
segments.s652-g14: ز introduces a causal/ablative idea 'from/because of'; دیدار او = 'her sight' or 'the seeing of her' (دیدار used poetically for 'view/sight'); شهر is the subject 'the city/town' and پر after it means 'full (of)'. گفت و گوی is a nominal compound meaning 'talk and conversation' (both words are nouns here). Together: 'Because of seeing her, the town was full of talk.'
segments.s652-g15: یکی کودک آمدش is the simple narrative clause 'a certain child came to her': یکی here functions as 'a certain' (indefinite), کودک 'child', آمدش is آمد + enclitic ـش = 'came to her/him' (past tense with 3rd‑person object). خورشید چهر is a postpositive descriptive tag 'sun-faced' or 'with a face like the sun' describing the child's visage (common poetic epithet where خورشید qualifies چهر).
segments.s652-g16: ز here introduces the comparative 'than' (classical usage: ز = 'than' or 'from' in comparisons): 'ز ناهید تابنده‌تر' = 'brighter than Nahīd (Venus)'. تابنده‌تر is the comparative form of تابنده 'shining' (the -تر ending makes it 'more shining'). بر سپهر adds the locative/comitative image 'upon the firmament/sky'.
segments.s652-g193: The phrase is a compact clause: adjective + noun + verb + prepositional phrase. 'گران بی‌خرد' places an emphatic adjective before the noun ('a grievously/weightily foolish [person]'). 'سر بپیچد' is a third-person present-form verb (بـ- prefix + پیچی + -د) used as a general statement 'turns his head' or 'turns away'; the verb takes 'سر' (head) as its object. 'ز داد' is a short prepositional phrase (ز = 'from') showing the source or object of turning: 'away from justice.'
segments.s652-g194: 'به دشنام او' functions adverbially ('to/at his insult' — i.e., in reply to his cursing). The predicate is an impersonal negative: 'لب نباید گشاد' literally 'the lip should not be opened.' 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'one must not / it is not fitting to,' and 'گشاد' (here adjectival/predicative) describes the state 'open/gaping' after the verb 'لب' (lip) — idiomatically 'do not speak / do not answer with a curse.'
segments.s652-g195: 'که' introduces an explanatory clause ('for / that'). 'دشنام او' = 'his insult.' 'ویژه' is an attributive adjective meaning 'special/directed specifically,' placed before 'دشنام' to mark that the insult is pointedly toward 'ما' (us). The sentence ends with the copula 'ست' ('is'), so the structure is '[That] his insult is especially our insult.'
segments.s652-g196: This is a short rhetorical question. 'کجا' here functions interrogatively/rhetorically ('how/where/how could it be') and 'از' introduces the domain or concern ('with / concerning'). The coordinated nouns 'پی و خون و اندام ما' list what might be affected ('lineage/foot/track' — here 'پی' is used figuratively for lineage or concern — plus 'blood' and 'body') and the clause closes again with the copula 'ست' ('is'), implying 'how could it be of our lineage, blood, or body?'
segments.s652-g197: 'تو' is the explicit vocative/subject 'you.' The two coordinated imperatives/commands follow: the clause calls to action toward the army. 'لشکر بیارای' — here the verb form is an imperative or jussive directing the addressee to 'array/prepare the host' (the supplied senses list doesn't include the verbal sense, so the lexical choice is expressed as an imperative command). 'و بر ساز جنگ' is a second coordinated command: 'بر' = 'against/for' + 'ساز' used as 'prepare/means' (literally 'make the instruments/means of war') and 'جنگ' = 'war.' Together: 'Array your army and make ready the means of war.'
segments.s652-g198: 'مدارا کن' is an imperative 'be tolerant / practice moderation' (مدارا + the imperative auxiliary 'کن'). 'اندر میان' is an adverbial phrase 'in the midst (of things / battle).' 'با درنگ' modifies the imperative with manner: 'with deliberation / without haste' — 'درنگ' here is used positively as deliberate pause or carefulness. The whole segment is an instruction to act with restraint and deliberation in the middle of action.
segments.s652-g199: 'ور‌ای' is the classical conditional particle (if/should) used here; it may be followed by a vocative sense in older styles but functions chiefly as 'if.' 'دون' is a substantive adjective meaning 'a low or ignoble person.' 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; 'تنگ اندر آید سخن' is idiomatic: 'tنگ' = 'tight/pressing' and 'آید سخن' = 'speech comes' — together 'when words become constrained' or more contextually 'when the situation becomes pressing / the battle tightens and someone speaks.' The clause describes the condition under which the following prohibition applies.
segments.s652-g200: 'به جنگ اندرون' is a locative/adverbial phrase 'within the battle / in the interior of the fight.' The main admonition is a negative imperative: 'هیچ تندی مکن' — 'do not make any harshness.' 'هیچ' negates 'تندی' (harshness, fierceness) and 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not.' So the clause commands complete avoidance of harshness while within battle.
segments.s652-g217: This clause states a subject followed by a past copula and three adjectives. 'عزیزی' is the subject ('a beloved, dear one'). 'بود' is the past tense of 'to be' (here 'was'). The three adjectives 'زار و خوار و نژند' are coordinated by 'و' and list contrary or humiliating qualities of that subject.
segments.s652-g218: This follow-up clause explains a surprising outcome. 'گزیده' is a passive/adjectival form meaning 'chosen/selected'. 'به شاهی' means 'to/for kingship' (here 'kingship' as the goal). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/by', and 'چرخ بلند' ('the high/lofty Wheel/Heaven') is the agent/source (the celestial Fate). Word order places the result 'گزیده به شاهی' before the source 'ز چرخ بلند'.
segments.s652-g219: 'بدین داستان زد' is an idiomatic poetic phrasing: 'بدین' = 'to this' (به + این) introduces the target of speaking; 'داستان' = 'tale/story'; 'زد' is used idiomatically here for 'struck forth' = 'uttered/related' (classical usage 'زدن' = to utter/tell a tale). 'یکی مهرنوش' = 'a certain Mehrnush' (یکی as an indefinite 'a certain'). Note: the input tokenization splits 'بدین' oddly; grammatically treat it as 'to this'.
segments.s652-g220: This is a nominal phrase describing Mehrnush. 'پرستار' is a descriptor (here 'devoted attendant/guardian' or 'worshipper' depending on nuance). 'با هوش' is an instrumental/qualificative phrase 'with intelligence' (i.e. 'clever'), and 'پشمینه‌پوش' is a compound adjective 'wool-clad' (literally 'wearing a woolen coat'); the three items are coordinately describing the same person.
segments.s652-g221: 'که' introduces a clause ('that / who'), and 'هرکو' is the classical fused form of 'هر که' meaning 'whoever'. 'به مرگ پدر گشت شاد' literally 'became happy at the father's death' — 'گشت' is past of 'شدن' (became) and follows the circumstantial phrase 'به مرگ پدر'. Word order is typical classical Persian: circumstantial phrase + verb + predicative adjective 'شاد'.
segments.s652-g222: 'ورا' = 'to him' (indirect object or dative). 'رامش و زندگانی' are the two nouns wished upon him ('peace/tranquility and life'). 'مباد' is a classical optative/jussive in the negative sense meaning 'may it not be' or 'may he not have'. Together the phrase expresses a wish that such a person have neither peace nor life.
segments.s652-g223: 'تو' is the addressee 'you'. 'از تیرگی روشنایی مجوی' is a prohibition: 'از ... مجوی' = 'do not seek from ...'. 'مجوی' is the negative present-subjunctive / jussive form used for prohibition ('do not seek'). Word order is topical: source 'from darkness' before the object 'light'.
segments.s652-g224: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause. 'با آتش آب' places two elements together ('water with fire'), 'اندر آید' = poetic 'comes into' (اندر = in/into; آید = comes), and 'به جوی' 'to/into the stream' is the goal. The line states the comparison/imagery: the sought thing is as incompatible as water and fire coming together into a stream (the poetic juxtaposition underlines impossibility or absurdity).
segments.s652-g17: ورا is the enclitic object pronoun meaning 'to him' (placed before the name phrase). نامور is a noun/adjective 'renowned' used as the name/epithet. خواندی is a past-tense (2nd or 3rd-person past in older Persian narrative) of خواندن in the sense 'to call/name'; here it functions as '(they) called (him)'. نوش‌زاد is an appositive noun 'newborn' placed after the verb phrase to qualify the named child.
segments.s652-g18: نجستی is a past form of جَستَن (to spring/leap) used here in the sense 'sprang' (poetic past). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ناز (here 'delicacy/affection/pampering') names the quality from which the action issues. از is the ordinary preposition 'from/out of' linking to برش, which here functions as 'his side/profile' (a noun meaning the surface/section of him). تندباد is the noun 'gale' or 'gust' that is the agent/phenomenon invoked; word order in Persian places the verb (نجستی) before the prepositional phrase that explains source/cause.
segments.s652-g19: ببالید is the past/imperfective poetic form of بالیدن used here as 'grew/grew proud/flourished.' برسان in this context functions idiomatically as 'reached up to / attained' (derived from رساندن 'to bring/reach'). سرو سهی is a set phrase: سرو 'cypress' (a common simile for a tall elegant person) with سهی 'dark/black' as a poetic epithet; the adjective follows the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s652-g20: هنرمند here is an adjective meaning 'skillful, artful' (not necessarily 'artist'); it modifies the implied subject. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. زیبای is an adjectival form 'beautiful' modifying the same subject. شاهنشهی is a genitive/epithet 'of kingship/imperial', forming a compound that characterizes his royal bearing; the noun phrase order groups qualities after the main verb.
segments.s652-g21: چو is the subordinating particle 'when/as' introducing a clause. دوزخ is the noun 'hell' as the direct object of بدانست; بدانست is past 'knew/realized' (بـ- prefix + دانست), so the clause reads 'when he knew/understood hell and the way to paradise.' و links دو مفردات (دوزخ and راه). راه بهشت follows the usual noun + modifier order: 'the way of Paradise.'
segments.s652-g22: This segment lists objects/authorities he recognized: عزیز is an epithet 'esteemed/beloved' used as a title; و is 'and'; مسیح is 'the Messiah/Christ' (here the religious figure); another و connects to ره 'way' (here used as 'path'); زردهشت is a Persian proper name for Zoroaster (the founder of Zoroastrianism). The sequence names both Christian and Zoroastrian religious references as alternatives/companions.
segments.s652-g23: نیامد is a negated past 'did not come' or idiomatically 'did not happen/was not destined'; in older Persian narratives it often means 'he did not (meet some fate)'. همی‌زند is a present/imperfective poetic form meaning 'he strikes/struck/acts continuously' (here part of a juxtaposed predicate); و is 'and'. استش is the contraction of است + ش (est + -esh) meaning 'it is for him/it is his' or 'it is proper'; درست is an adjective/adverb 'correct/right/proper'. Together the clause conveys that something did not befall him and that it was proper/right.
segments.s652-g24: دو رخ را is the direct object marker construction: دو رخ 'two faces' (or 'both cheeks/sides') with را marking it as definite object. به is the preposition 'with/by'; آب is 'water'; مسیحا is the vocative/genitive-like or poetic form meaning 'of the Messiah' or 'Messiah's (water)' (here 'the water of Christ' = baptismal water). بشست is the past tense verb 'washed' (3rd-person singular); Persian word order puts the object (دو رخ را) before the verb and the instrument/agent phrase (به آب مسیحا) before the verb as well.
segments.s652-g121: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خاشاک ناچیز is a noun + adjective ('insignificant chaff') with the adjective following the noun as is normal in Persian. تا here means 'as far as / up to', linking the small thing to the large examples (شیر و پیل). The coordinate و simply joins شیر and پیل.
segments.s652-g122: ز again = 'from'. گرد پی مور is a compact classical phrase: گرد = 'dust' and پی = 'foot/track', so 'the dust of the ant's track' (the ezāfe is implied/omitted in poetry). تا = 'as far as / even to', followed by رود نیل 'the Nile (river)', where رود is 'river' and نیل specifies which river.
segments.s652-g123: همه (subject 'all/everyone') + زیر فرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'under the command of Yezdan (God)'. بود is the past copula 'was/were' (classical style often uses بود after collective subjects).
segments.s652-g124: وگر is a concessive 'but if / and if'. در دم = 'in a moment' (دم = moment), an idiom meaning 'at once' or 'for an instant'. سنگ و سندان 'stone and anvil' are the items named, and بود is again the past copula 'were/was'. The clause is hypothetical/concessive.
segments.s652-g125: نه negates the clause ('no / not'). فرمان او را 'his command' uses را as the definite/object marker; classical word order places او after فرمان. کرانه پدید literally 'an edge appeared' — here 'پدید' = 'appear/manifest', so the sense is 'no limit/boundary of his command appeared' → 'his command had no limit.'
segments.s652-g126: This is the second negative clause introduced by نه. ز + (و) here functions as the classical contraction 'زو' = 'از او' ('from him'); in the manuscript form the clitic is written as و attached to ز. پادشاهی is 'kingship/sovereignty' and بخواهد is a modal/optative (classical present/future sense 'would/will be able / one would wish to'), while برید (from بردن) here means 'take (away)' or 'sever'. Together: 'nor (could) take kingship from him.'
segments.s652-g127: بدانستم is first-person past 'I knew/considered' (here 'I regarded / I judged'). این نامه is 'this letter' and ناپسند is an adjective 'displeasing / unacceptable' modifying نامه. The whole small clause means 'I found this letter displeasing.'
segments.s652-g128: که is the relative/conjunction 'that/which'. آمد is the past 'came' and ز + فرزند = 'from a son/descendant' (classical phrasing). چندین گزند = 'many / several harms' (چندین = many/several, گزند = harm), so the clause explains that the letter came from a son and brought many harms.
segments.s652-g209: 'گرامی' is a substantive meaning 'an honored/dear one' and 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'that/who...'. The clause 'خواری کند' uses 'کند' (3rd-person singular present) with 'خواری' (humiliation) to mean 'be humiliated' (i.e., 'that he suffer humiliation'), and 'آرزوی' at the end names the thing being wished — 'the wish that...'. Word order is noun + relative clause + the noun 'آرزوی' (the wish) that refers back to that clause.
segments.s652-g210: 'نشاید' is an impersonal predicate meaning 'it is not fitting/proper'. The infinitival/verbal phrase 'جدا کرد' (literally 'to make separate / to separate') expresses the action that would be improper. 'او را' is the object ('him') with the object marker 'را', and 'ز خوی' means 'from his khwī' — here 'خوی' = disposition/habit/nature. Together: 'It is not fitting to separate him from his nature.'
segments.s652-g211: 'یکی' here means 'a certain' (introducing an individual). 'ارجمندی' names his rank/quality ('nobility; dignity') and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The two following words 'کشته خوار' are past-participial/adjectival descriptors: 'killed/slain' and 'despised/humbled', giving 'a certain noble was slain and despised.'
segments.s652-g212: 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. 'با شاه گیتی' = 'with the king of the world' (با = with; شاه گیتی = 'king of the world'), and 'کند کارزار' = 'engages/makes battle' where 'کند' is 3rd-person singular present of کردن used to mean 'to wage' and 'کارزار' is 'battle'. The clause means 'when he fought a battle with the king of the world.'
segments.s652-g213: 'تواز' literally 'from you'/'by you' frames the act 'کشتن او' ('killing him') as done by you; the cluster 'مدار ایچ باک' is a negative admonition: 'مدار' here functions as 'do not hold / do not have', 'ایچ' = 'nothing/not at all', and 'باک' = 'fear'. So the whole segment means 'do not have any fear about your killing of him' — i.e., 'do not be afraid to kill him.'
segments.s652-g214: 'چوخون' is a simile 'like blood' qualifying the following noun. 'سرخویش' = سر + خویش, 'his own ruddy head' (سرخو = ruddy/red; خویش = his own). 'گیرد' (3rd-sg. present of گرفتن) here means 'is taken' or 'is seized/carried' (can be read as 'may be taken'). 'به خاک' = 'to the earth/dust'. So: 'though his ruddy head be taken to the dust like blood.'
segments.s652-g215: 'سوی' = 'toward'; 'کیش قیصر' is a genitive phrase 'the creed/sect of Caesar' (i.e., Caesar's religion/faith); 'گراید' is the 3rd-person singular present of گردیدن/گراییدن meaning 'turns/leans toward'; and 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/indeed'. The segment states 'he turns toward Caesar's creed (continually).'
segments.s652-g216: 'ز' = 'from' (poetic), 'دیهیم ما' = 'our rural place/our village' (دیهیم used with ما = our), 'سر' = 'head' (subject), and 'بتابدهمی' is built on تابیدن 'to shine' with the prefix/particle بِـ and enclitic 'همی', giving a present/future sense 'will shine' or 'shines continually.' The clause thus says 'from our village his head will shine' (i.e., his head/face will gleam or be exalted among us).
segments.s652-g177: سزای + noun in genitive (دهش‌گر) = “the recompense/desert of the giver.” نیایش بدی is a noun phrase where بدی qualifies نیایش as an ‘evil prayer’ or ‘prayer for harm.’ Word order is poetic (possessive + head + modifier) but readable as “the giver’s reward: an evil prayer.”
segments.s652-g178: مرا (here written as م + را) marks the experiencer/object ‘me’ (the person affected). بر فزونی فزایش بدی literally = “upon (بر) increase (فزونی) [of] increase (فزایش) harm (بدی)” — a compressed poetic way to say he inflicted harm which grew and grew; بدی is the harm done. The verb is implicit from context (he did/placed).
segments.s652-g179: گر is the conditional ‘if.’ از پشت من = ‘from my back’ (preposition + noun + possessor). رفت is the past of رفتن ‘went/left’ used here of the drop departing; یک قطره آب is the logical subject/thing that left and is postposed for poetic emphasis.
segments.s652-g180: به جای دگر = ‘to/into another place.’ یافته is the past participle/verb ‘has found’ (here used as main verb). جای خواب = ‘a place of sleep/rest’ (literal ‘place of sleep’) — together: ‘has found another place to rest.’ Word order reflects poetic compression: prepositional phrase + verb + purpose phrase.
segments.s652-g181: چو = ‘when.’ بیدار شد = ‘became awake’ (past, i.e. ‘I woke’). دشمن آمد = ‘the enemy came’ and مرا (m + را) marks that the arrival was directed at ‘me’ — the object/experiencer placed after the verb for emphasis, as is common in epic/persian poetic style.
segments.s652-g182: بترسم is a first‑person present/subjunctive-like form ‘I fear’ (used to introduce a feared outcome). که introduces the subordinate clause. رنج از من آمد = ‘suffering/trouble may come from/upon me’ (آمد here expresses the (potential) coming of trouble), and again مرا marks the experiencer ‘me’ — the whole clause = ‘I fear that suffering will befall me.’
segments.s652-g183: وگر = ‘if’ (conditional). گاه here is temporal/adverbial ‘at times’ or ‘occasionally.’ خشم جهاندار = ‘the anger of the sovereign/king’ (possessive order), and نیست = ‘is not / does not exist’ — literal: ‘if at times the king’s anger is not there.’
segments.s652-g184: مرا (m + را) begins an experience‑oriented construction meaning roughly ‘for me there is no…’ or ‘I have no…’. از چنین کار تیمار نیست = ‘there is no care/concern from/about such a deed’ → idiomatically ‘I take no heed/care for such a matter.’ تیمار = ‘care/solicitude.’ The negation نیست applies to the whole clause.
segments.s652-g73: چنین 'thus/so' (adverb) + گفت (past tense 'said') + گوینده پارسی 'the Persian speaker/narrator'. The phrase is a conventional narrative formula — 'چنین گفت گوینده پارسی' = 'thus said the Persian narrator' — with the verb گفت placed between the adverb and the agent for poetic effect.
segments.s652-g74: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); بگذشت is the past, impersonal/3rd-sg sense 'passed' used with a time-measure (سال). از برش is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'over him' / 'since him' (از + بر + -ش 'his'), and چار (here as part of چار سی) is the numeral 'four' used in the archaic numeral ordering to form 'thirty-four'.
segments.s652-g75: The clause begins with که (that) and uses the correlative relative 'هر کس که' = 'whoever'. بر here means 'against' and takes the noun دادگر 'the just' as its object; دشمنست is the archaic fused copula-form meaning 'is an enemy'. The word order and doubled که are typical of literary Persian: 'that whoever is against the just is an enemy.'
segments.s652-g76: نه negates the following predicate: 'not of the people/race.' مردم نژادست is a predicate where نژادست (literally 'is of the race/lineage') states belonging, and the following که introduces an explanatory clause 'that (is) Ahriman' — i.e. 'he is not human; he is Ahriman.'
segments.s652-g77: هم 'also' + از 'from' marks origin; نوش‌زاد here means 'newly made/newly born' (a poetic way to say recent origin). آمد is past 'came' and the noun phrase این داستان is the theme ('this story'); the verb comes before the noun for poetic inversion: 'also from a fresh source came this story.'
segments.s652-g78: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause. یاد آمد is an idiomatic combination meaning 'it was recalled / came to memory' (یاد as 'remembrance' + آمد 'came'). از گفته باستان 'from ancient sayings/accounts' gives the source: از + گفته (reports/sayings) + باستان (ancient).
segments.s652-g79: چو 'when' + بشنید (past 3rd-sg 'he heard') introduces a temporal clause. فرزند کسری 'the son of Khosrow' is the experiencer; the following که begins the content of what he heard ('that the throne ...'). Note the poetic word order where the verb بشنید precedes the subject for emphasis.
segments.s652-g80: بپردخت is a past verb form (literary) indicating action toward the throne (here 'placed/caused to be placed' or 'paid to the throne'); زان is a demonstrative 'from/thereof' (= 'that'); خسروانی is adjectival 'royal / Khosrowian'; درخت is the noun 'tree' (used here as a symbolic object). Together the phrase describes placing that royal tree upon/at the throne.
segments.s652-g137: زمین‌گر is a poetic conditional construction: زمین + -گر (= “if the earth…”). گشاده کند is a present/subjunctive-style clause (گشاده = 'opened', کند = 3sg verbal form 'does/would do'), so together “if the earth should open.” راز خویش = راز + خوی + ش (possessive enclitic); here خویش functions as 'its own/its', so literally 'its secret.'
segments.s652-g138: بپیماید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive with the poetic prefix بـ (here rendering a conditional/future sense: 'would traverse/measure'). آغاز و انجام خویش = 'its beginning and its end' (آغاز = beginning, انجام = completion/end) with خویش again the possessive 'its.' The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s652-g139: کنارش = کنار + ش, here 'beside him' (کنار used as 'beside/next to'). The predicate is پر از تاجداران بود ('was full of crowned ones'); پر از is the idiom 'full of', تاجداران (pl.) = 'crowned ones/sovereigns', and بود is the past copula 'was' (poetic word order places the locative first).
segments.s652-g140: برش here functions as بر + ش (on/upon him/it) in the sense 'on him'; پر ز خون سواران بود = 'was full of the blood of the horsemen' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic variant of از 'of/from', خون = blood, سواران = horsemen, بود = 'was'). The line uses an inverted, compact poetic order.
segments.s652-g141: This is inverted poetic order: normally 'دامنش پر از مرد دانا بود' — دامنش (دامن + ش) 'his hem/skirt' is the possessor, پر از = 'full of', مرد دانا = 'wise men' (مرد = men, دانا = wise), and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s652-g142: جیب پیراهنش = 'the pocket of his shirt' (جیب = pocket, پیراهن = shirt/tunic, ش = possessive). The phrase is rendered 'پر از خوب رخ' = 'full of fair-faced (good-faced) ones' where خوب رخ is an adjectival phrase 'beautiful-faced'. Again, poetically condensed word order places the predicate around the noun.
segments.s652-g143: This is a pair of rhetorical questions. چه افسر نهی بر سرت = 'what helmet do you place upon your head?' (چه = 'what', افسر = helmet, نهی functions as 2nd-person singular present of نهادن 'you place/put' in classical/poetic usage). بر چه ترگ = 'upon what rag/shred' (بر = 'on/upon', ترگ = 'rag/shred'). The phrasing is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s652-g144: بدو = 'unto him' (to him/onto him), بگذرد is 3sg present/subjunctive of گذشتن 'to pass' with the poetic b‑ prefix, so بدو بگذرد = 'falls upon him' or 'passes onto him'. زخم پیکان مرگ = 'the wound of the arrow of death' (زخم = wound, پیکان = arrow, مرگ = death). Together: 'upon him passes the wound of the arrow of death.'
segments.s652-g65: ز من بشنو is a short imperative/request phrasing meaning “hear from me” (ز = poetic ‘from’, من = object ‘me’ placed after the preposition). این داستان سر به سر is a noun phrase meaning “this tale, head-to-head,” where سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning “completely / from beginning to end.” Word order is preposition + pronoun (ز من) then verb (بشنو), then the direct object phrase.
segments.s652-g66: بگویم is a first-person verb form meaning “I will say / I shall tell” (classical Persian uses this present/subjunctive form for a hortatory declaration). The object follows as تو را (you + definite/object marker را). The vocative ای پسر (‘O youth/son’) follows the object; در بدر is a locative phrase “in/at Badr” (بدر here a proper name). Note: the supplied sense list for بگویم did not include ‘say’, so sense is marked -1.
segments.s652-g67: چو is the archaic conjunction “as / like / when” introducing the clause; گفتار here carries the sense of ‘style/manner of speaking’ (not merely ‘speech’), so چو گفتار دهقان بیاراستم literally = “when/like (I) fashioned my speech in the dehqan’s manner.” بیاراستم is a past first-person verb ‘I adorned/arranged/fashioned’ describing a completed action.
segments.s652-g68: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase ‘به + این’ meaning ‘by this / with this’. خویشتن را is the emphatic object ‘myself’ with the object marker را. نشان is a noun ‘sign/token’ functioning as the object of خواستم; خواستم is the past-tense first-person ‘I wished/desired/sought’. The whole clause = “by this I wished for a token/showing of myself” i.e. “I sought to make myself known.” Note: the segmented token دین is actually part of بدین here and cannot be used with the provided senses, so it is marked -1.
segments.s652-g69: که introduces a subordinate/purposive clause here (‘so that’/‘that’). ماند is a present/past-form meaning ‘remain / be left’ and governs ز من یادگاری (‘a memento of me’), with چنین qualifying it as ‘such’ or ‘thus’. The sequence gives the purpose/result: “that such a memento remain of me.”
segments.s652-g70: بدان is the contracted form of به + آن (‘to/for that’); آفرین here is the noun/exclamation ‘praise / acclamation’. کو is an archaic interrogative (here effectively ‘who/which will’) and کند is the present/subjunctive/optative third-person singular of کردن ‘to do/make’. The clause asks rhetorically “to that praise, who will do/say ‘afrin’?” — i.e. “who will call it worthy of praise?” Some segment tokens were split by the input (دان, کو) in ways that make their provided sense lists inapplicable, so those are marked -1.
segments.s652-g71: پس از مرگ is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘after death’ (پس used here as ‘after’). بر من here means ‘concerning me / as for me’. که introduces the content clause ‘that I am the narrator.’ گوینده‌ام is a predicative nominal with the first-person enclitic -ام (‘I am [the] narrator’).
segments.s652-g72: بدین نام = ‘by this name’ (به + این = بدین used adverbially). جاوید is an epithet meaning ‘eternal’, and جوینده‌ام is ‘I am the seeker’ (جوینده + enclitic -ام). The phrase states a wish/label after death: ‘by this name (call me) the eternal seeker.’ As above, the input split the fused particle, so دین is treated as not matching its provided senses and marked -1.
segments.s652-g1: This is a protasis introduced by the conditional particle اگر (if). شاه (king) is the noun subject and دیدی is a classical/poetic perfect form used here in a conditional sense ("if you meet/see"). وگر (و + گر) introduces an alternative clause ("or if") linking the following noun زیردست (a subordinate/retainer) as another possible person encountered.
segments.s652-g2: وگر again marks an alternative ('or if'). پاکدل is an adjectival compound placed before the noun مرد (man/person) and یزدان‌پرست is a postpositive compound adjective meaning 'one who worships God' — the three words form a single noun phrase 'a pure‑hearted, God‑worshipping man.'
segments.s652-g3: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative construction: چنان (thus/so) + دان (consider/hold). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). چاره نباشد is a negative existential/optative phrase ('there is no remedy' / 'there is no need'); here نباشد is used in the classical optative/subjunctive sense. ز introduces the source/area ('from/of') and جفت ('pair/mate') names the sphere from which no remedy/need exists.
segments.s652-g4: This is the complement of ز in the previous clause: a coordinated list of verbal nouns/infinitives and a nominal phrase. پوشیدن and خورد are infinitival nouns ('wearing/clothing' and 'eating/food') joined by و, and جای نهفت is a noun (جای 'place') qualified by the adjective نهفت ('hidden/concealed') placed after it — together: 'for clothing, eating, and a hidden place.'
segments.s652-g5: A conditional clause: اگر introduces the condition, پارسا (pious) is the predicate adjective and باشد is the classical subjunctive/present of 'to be' used in the protasis ('if he be/if he is'). و links رای‌زن (literally 'opiner/counsellor') as an additional quality of the same person.
segments.s652-g6: یکی is an indefinite/numeral intensifier ('one, a single') placed before the noun گنج ('treasure') to stress uniqueness. باشد again is the subjunctive/apodotic copula ('is/should be'). براگنده (an adjective/adverb 'openly, manifest') followed by زن — here زن functions as a verbal imperative/inflected verb ('strike/make') in poetic usage — yields a compound command-like phrase meaning 'make it manifest / spread it widely.'
segments.s652-g7: بویژه ('especially') foregrounds the clause introduced by که. باشد is again the subjunctive/copula ('if he is/is being'), and the phrase به بالا بلند uses the preposition به + spatial nouns بالا ('above') and بلند ('lofty/tall') to describe elevated position — literally 'in/at the high, lofty place.'
segments.s652-g8: فروهشته is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'scattered' or 'cast down.' تا is a limitative preposition ('as far as/until'), پای is 'foot/base,' مشکین is an attributive adjective here ('dark, black' — often poetically 'musk‑coloured'), and کمند is a concrete noun ('noose/loop, snare'). Together they form a spatial image: 'scattered down to the foot like a dark noose.'
segments.s652-g57: This is a correlative negative construction with نه ... نه ...: the first نه negates the following clause. زو (ز + او) is a poetic clitic meaning “from him,” followed by بار “load/burden.” باید که یابد is a periphrastic subordinate clause (باید + که + verb) meaning ‘there is need that he find’ or more idiomatically ‘to obtain.’ Word order puts the necessity phrase between the two negations: ‘neither ... nor ...’.
segments.s652-g58: ز خاکش is a prepositional phrase ‘from his dust’ (ز + possessive clitic). بود is the past copula (‘was’/‘there was’), placed before the nominal predicate for poetic inversion; زندگانی و مرگ is the coordinated predicate ‘life and death.’ The construction therefore literally reads ‘from his dust were life and death.’
segments.s652-g59: یکی here is the indefinite article/particle ‘a certain’ (not the numeral emphasizing one); داستان کردم uses کردن in the past (کردم) to mean ‘I made/told a story.’ از + نوش‌زاد marks the topic/subject matter ‘about a newborn.’ Word order is Subject–Object–Verb with the oblique object introduced by از.
segments.s652-g60: نگه کن is an imperative compound: نگه (look/watch) + کن (do) = ‘pay attention’ or ‘take heed.’ مگر here functions as ‘lest’/‘unless’ introducing a negative-subjunctive warning. نپیچی is the negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن (‘turn’): ن- + پیچی (subjunctive) = ‘do not turn.’ ز داد = ‘from justice’ (ز = from, داد = justice). Together: ‘Take heed lest your head turn away from justice.’
segments.s652-g61: اگر introduces a conditional clause ‘if.’ چرخ را uses را as the definite/object marker on چرخ (‘the Wheel’ = fate/fortune). کوش is a verb form (imperative/subjunctive stem meaning ‘strive/endeavor’). صدری is an adjective/attribute here (pertaining to a صدر / front/head), and بدی in classical usage is an archaic 2nd-person verbal form (you give/you make); the clause therefore reads roughly ‘if you strive against the Wheel / if you set your effort upon the Wheel.’ Note the poetic economy of classical Persian verbs and word-order (object/topic before the verb).
segments.s652-g62: همانا is a discourse marker ‘verily/indeed.’ که introduces the content clause. صدریش is صدر + possessive clitic (his/its front/forepart). کسری is the proper name ‘Kisra’ (Khosrow) used here as a metaphorical standard; بدی again is the archaic 2nd-person verb-form (you make/give). The line asserts as a consequence: ‘indeed you would make its front like a Kisra’s,’ using inversion and compact classical verbal forms for effect.
segments.s652-g63: This is a rhetorical interrogative: پسر ‘the son’ is the topic; سر چرا پیچد is ‘why should (he) turn his head’ — چرا is ‘why,’ پیچد is present/subjunctive of پیچیدن ‘to turn.’ از راه اوی is the prepositional phrase ‘from his way/path,’ with اوی an archaic possessive/enclitic pronoun ‘his.’ Word order is topicalizing the subject before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s652-g64: نشست is the past of نشستن (‘he sat’/‘he settled’) used here to mean he waited or took a seat. که introduces a subordinate/purpose or result clause. جوید is the present/subjunctive of جستن/جویدن ‘to seek/watch for’ and here means ‘to await/see when.’ ابر گاه اوی is a compound nominal ‘cloud-time of him’ — i.e. the season/time of clouds/rain for him — with اوی = ‘his.’ The whole gives the reason/circumstance: he sat, awaiting when the clouds would come to him.
segments.s652-g233: سپاهی (subject) is a noun 'army/warriors' placed at the start; که هستند is a relative clause ('who are') with هستند as the present indicative plural of بودن. با introduces an accompaniment or means ('with'); نوش زاد is a compound-like phrase meaning 'supplied with drink/provisions' (nouns follow the preposition). The whole segment = 'an army who are supplied with drink'.
segments.s652-g234: کجا starts an interrogative/relative adverb 'where/how'; سر به پیچند is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to turn (one's) head' where پیچند is the present/subjunctive/imperative-form root used here for 'turn' (third-person plural form); چندین 'often/several' modifies the action; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and داد here means 'justice' (abstract noun). The clause asks rhetorically where such troops would turn their heads with respect to justice.
segments.s652-g235: تو is the vocative/address 'you'; آن را is a topically marked object 'that (thing)'; جز از means 'except/from nothing but'; باد و بازی is a fixed pairing 'wind and play' i.e. 'mere wind and sport' (a formula meaning insubstantial); مدان is an archaic/imperfective imperative/hortative form meaning 'know' (comparable to classical بداند/بدان).
segments.s652-g236: گزاف زنان is a nominal phrase meaning 'empty/groundless boasting' (گزاف = groundless/excessive; زنان here functions like a participial/agentive element derived from زدن — 'those who utter/strike', i.e. 'boasters'). بود is the past/copular verb 'was'. و رای بدان links the next noun رای ('opinion, counsel') to 'that' with the prepositional-like prefix ب + دان (here دان = 'know' or as attached particle forms 'بدان'/'on that'); syntactically the phrase says 'and that was their counsel/opinion.'
segments.s652-g237: هران کس = 'anyone/each one' (هر + آن contracted), که introduces a relative clause. ترساست is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'is timid/faint‑hearted' (ترسا = fearful, with -است copula). از لشکرش literally 'from his army/host' here functions as 'in his host' or 'among his troops' (از used partitive).
segments.s652-g238: همی is an adverb meaning 'always/still' placed before the verb for emphasis. از پی is a phrase 'because of / following' (from + after), here 'on account of' or 'in pursuit of'; کیش is 'creed/sect' and is the motive given. پیچد is the verb 'turns'/'twists' (third-person singular form used here). سرش ('his head') is the object of پیچد ('turns his head'). The clause states the habitual reaction of such men.
segments.s652-g239: چنینست is a fixed copular clause 'it is thus/so'; کیش مسیحا is a genitive construction 'the creed of the Messiah' (کیش governed by مسیحا). که introduces a result/consequence clause; دم here is a noun 'a breath/moment' that begins the following nominal phrase. The whole sets up a general characterization of that creed.
segments.s652-g240: This clause describes the effect: تیز modifies an implied utterance ('a sharp [word/utterance]'); زنی in this context functions as a noun/gerund meaning 'a single utterance/strike' (the provided lemma senses list lacks this nuance, so sense identification is marked below as unavailable). و گرددد is the copular/verb sequence 'and becomes' (گردد = 'become'); کسی = 'someone'; زو = 'from it' (poetic contraction of ز + او); دژم = 'sullen/hostile'. Together: 'a sharp utterance and someone becomes hostile because of it.'
segments.s652-g113: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشت is a past-tense of گشتن 'to become/turn' and with the following phrase means 'became / took on' (i.e. 'took hold'). آن سخن = 'that speech'. بر دلش 'upon his heart' is a locative phrase (بر + دل + possessive clitic ش). جای گیر is a light verbal/adjectival phrase meaning 'take a place / become fixed' (literally 'place-taking'), so the whole segment reads 'when that speech became fixed on his heart' (= 'when that talk settled in his heart').
segments.s652-g114: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded' (literary use of فرمود). تا introduces the purposive/result clause 'so that / that'. نزد او = 'into his presence' (نزد = in the presence of). شد is a past/happen verb 'came / happened' (here 'came [to him]'). دبیر is the person brought: 'secretary / scribe'. Together: 'he ordered that a scribe come to him' (i.e. he summoned a scribe).
segments.s652-g115: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone / a certain one'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بنوشت is an older/polite past form meaning 'wrote' (ب + نوشت); the provided lemma senses do not include this verbal sense, so the appropriate sense is not in the list. با داغ و درد is an adverbial phrase 'with burning grief and (emotional) pain', giving the manner in which the letter was written.
segments.s652-g116: پرآژنگ رخ is a compact epithet: پرآژنگ (ornamented / richly colored) modifies رخ 'face' — 'a face adorned/painted'. لب پر از باد سرد: لب 'lip', پر 'full (of)' + از 'from/of' + باد سرد 'cold wind' — literally 'lips full of cold wind' (a poetic way to describe pale, chilled lips). Note that پر appears twice in different roles: as part of the compound پرآژنگ and as the adjective 'full (of)'.
segments.s652-g117: نخستین 'first' (adverb/ordinal). بران = بر + آن, here 'on that / upon Him' (anaphoric; 'on that [subject]'). آفرین is the noun 'acclamation / praise' (not the verb 'to create' here). گسترید is the past of گستراندن 'to spread / to pour forth'; together 'نخستین بران آفرین گسترید' = 'first he poured forth praise upon Him.'
segments.s652-g118: که introduces a relative/complement clause 'who/that'. چرخ و زمان و زمین are the coordinated objects 'the sky (heaven), and time, and the earth'. آفرید is the past tense 'created'. So the clause modifies the praised One: 'who created heaven, time, and earth.'
segments.s652-g119: نگارنده here functions as a poetic epithet for the Creator — literally 'the writer/painter/maker' — and governs the following list of celestial things he fashioned: هور (lit. 'light/splendor', here poetic for the sun or bright radiance), و کیوان 'and Saturn', و ماه 'and the moon'. The phrase names what the 'نگارنده' has fashioned.
segments.s652-g120: فروزنده is an adjectival/participial epithet 'illuminating / making bright'. فر is the divine/royal 'farr' (splendour or divine glory). و + دیهیم + و + گاه form a triad: دیهیم (literally 'rural / of the land' or 'the people of the land' in the senses given) and گاه 'time/occasion'. The segment strings epithets together: 'illuminator of (divine) glory, of the rural domain, and of time' — i.e. further attributes of the same Creator. (The line is highly compressed and poetic; نگارنده ... فروزنده links as a series of titles of the Creator.)
segments.s652-g321: تو گفتی is 2nd-person past (تو + گفتی) = “you said.” مَگر here functions as a hedge/introductory particle = “perhaps” or “may be,” introducing the reported clause. خاک جوشان شدست is a nominal+adjective + archaic perfective: خاک (subject ‘the earth/ground’) + جوشان (adjective ‘boiling, seething’) + شدست (poetic contraction of شده است, ‘has become’), so literally ‘the earth has become boiling’ and it is the content of what ‘you said.’
segments.s652-g322: هوا is the grammatical subject (‘the air/wind’). بر سر او is a prepositional phrase ‘upon/over his head’ (بر = on/upon, سر = head, او = him). خروشان is a predicative adjective ‘roaring/tumultuous’ and شدست (poetic شده است) is the copular/perfective ‘has become’ — together ‘the air has become roaring over him’ = ‘a roaring wind has risen above his head.’
segments.s652-g323: زره دار is a compound meaning ‘armor-bearing/armored’ (زره = armor). گردی here appears as a poetic/nominalizing element forming the referring phrase (‘one’/‘figure’), followed by the past verb بیامد (3rd‑person singular ‘came’) and the adjective دلیر (‘brave’), so the clause reads ‘an armored, brave one came.’ بیامد is simple past third person.
segments.s652-g324: This clause gives the name: کجا نام او بود is an inverted/question-like or poetic order meaning ‘what/which was his name’ or more simply ‘his name was…’ (اوبود = او + بود, ‘he/it was’). پیروز شیر is the proper name/epithet ‘Pirooz‑Shir’ (پیروز = victorious, شیر = lion). The whole functions as apposition identifying the arriving warrior.
segments.s652-g325: خروشید is a past intransitive verb ‘(there) resounded/roared/was a cry’ (3rd person). کای is a vocative particle ‘O,’ here addressing someone, followed by epithets نامور نوش‌زاد (‘renowned, newly‑born’). The sequence is a shouted address introduced by the verb of crying.
segments.s652-g326: سرت را uses the object-marker را after سرت (‘your head’), marking it as the direct object. پیچید is the past 3rd‑person of پیچیدن ‘to twist/turn.’ که here links the clause (relative/that) and should be read as ‘who/that’ or ‘that,’ and چونین = ‘thus/so.’ ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/by,’ and داد here is the noun ‘justice’ (rather than the verb ‘gave’), so ز داد = ‘by/for justice.’ Grammatically: ‘he twisted your head thus, by/for justice.’
segments.s652-g327: بگشتی is 2nd‑person past (you turned/you returned/you turned away). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ دین = ‘religion/faith,’ and کیومرثی is adjectival/relational ‘of Kiumarṯ’ (i.e., Kiumarth‑style faith). Together the clause says that the addressee departed/turned from the faith of Kiumarṯ.
segments.s652-g328: هم = ‘also/too’; از = ‘from’; راه = ‘way/path’; هوشنگ and طهمورثی name the legendary figures Hushang and Tahmūrath (طهمورثی is adjectival ‘of Tahmūrath’). The clause adds that the turning away was also from the paths/ways associated with Hushang and Tahmuras.
segments.s652-g313: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic). گرد سواران جوشنوران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'the ring/host of horsemen (who are) mail-clad'; گرد here indicates a circle/group (word order: modifier follows گرد). جوشنوران is an epithet attached to سواران (postpositive adjective/epithet), so the whole phrase gives the origin/agent for the action that follows.
segments.s652-g314: گراییدن is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'turned/turned toward' (functioning as the main verb); in this clause it carries the action and is followed by its object. گرزهای گران is the direct object (گرزهای = 'maces/clubs' in the plural with the Persian plural -های, and گران = 'heavy/weighty' postpositive), so the clause literally reads 'turned heavy maces.'
segments.s652-g315: دل سنگ خارا is a noun phrase 'the heart of flinty stone' (دل 'heart' modified by سنگ خارا 'stone, flinty/hard'), and همی‌بردرید is a progressive/continuous past verb (همی- prefix + بردرید from بریدن) meaning 'kept cutting/was cleaving.' The verb expresses repeated or continuous action directed at the preceding object phrase.
segments.s652-g316: کسی ... ندید is a negative existential construction meaning 'no one saw.' روی خورشید تابان is the object phrase 'the face of the shining sun' (روی 'face' followed by its genitive/modifier خورشید 'sun' and the adjective تابان 'shining'); ندید is the past negative of دیدن ('did not see').
segments.s652-g317: به قلب سپاه اندرون is a locative phrase: به + 'قلب سپاه' ('the heart/core of the army') with اندرون 'within/inner' specifying the interior part. نوش‌زاد ('newly-born' / 'fresh-made') functions as the noun/adjective identifying the individual who performs the action in the following segment.
segments.s652-g318: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'a certain' or 'one.' ترگ رومی is the object phrase 'a Roman rag/shred' (ترگ 'rag/shred' qualified by رومی 'of Rûm/Roman'). به سر برنهاد is the verb plus locative: برنهاد (past action 'placed/raised') with به سر ('onto the head'), so the clause reads 'a certain one placed a Roman rag on his head.' Word order is object before verb (SOV), typical of Persian.
segments.s652-g319: سپاهی بد is a noun plus adjective 'a bad/wicked soldier' (the adjective بد follows the noun). از جاثلقیان روم is a prepositional phrase 'from the jathliqians (catholicoi/bishops) of Rûm,' indicating origin or association; the whole phrase names or identifies the man just mentioned.
segments.s652-g320: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying the preceding noun. پیدا نبد is an archaic negative copular/verb phrase meaning 'was not visible / could not be seen' (پیدا 'visible' with the negative form نبد). از پی نعل بوم is a locational/instrumental phrase 'from the track/footprint of the horseshoe on the homeland' — together the relative clause says that no trace (from the horseshoe's track) could be seen on the native soil. Note the older negative form نبد rather than modern نبود/نبود.
segments.s652-g81: در کاخ is a prepositional phrase 'in the palace' (در + noun). بگشاد is an archaic past tense form of بگشودن ('to open' or 'to convene') in 3rd singular; Persian here follows a V S order so the verb (بگشاد) precedes its subject فرزند شاه ('the king's son').
segments.s652-g82: برو is a short discursive/temporal particle (thereupon/then) used in older Persian; انجمن شد is a passive/intransitive verbal phrase where انجمن ('assembly') is the noun and شد (3rd sg. past of شدن) indicates 'was formed/occurred'. فراوان modifies سپاه ('a plentiful/great host'); the whole clause means 'thereupon a great army was assembled.'
segments.s652-g83: کسی کو is an older relative construction meaning 'someone who' (کو here functions like an archaic relative 'که'). ز بند خرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the fetter/bond of reason' (ز + بند + خرد). جسته بود is a compound past (perfect) with the participle جسته ('escaped/leapt') plus the auxiliary بود ('had'), yielding 'had escaped'.
segments.s652-g84: به زندان indicates direction/place 'into/to prison' (به + noun). نوشین‌روان is an epithet/name 'Nushin-ravan' (literally 'sweet‑souled') modifying زندان. بسته is a past participle meaning 'bound/closed/put under restraint' and بود is the auxiliary in past ('had been'), giving the passive/past sense 'had been confined.'
segments.s652-g85: ز زندانها بندها is an inverted word order typical of poetry: 'from the prisons (ز زندانها) the fetters (بندها)'. برگرفت is a 3rd person singular past verb meaning 'lifted/removed/took away' — here 'removed the fetters from the prisons.'
segments.s652-g86: همه شهر is the subject 'the whole city' or 'everyone in the city'. ازو means 'on account of him' / 'because of him'. دست بر سر گرفت is an idiomatic construction 'to put the hand on the head' (دست + بر + سر) with گرفت (past of گرفتن) meaning 'they put/took' — here describing the populace's reaction (gesture of grief, respect, or intense feeling) directed toward him.
segments.s652-g87: به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/within the city's inner quarters' (به + شهر + اندرون). هرک is an archaic relative meaning 'whoever/every one who'; ترسا means 'a pious person' or 'religious man'. بدند is the old 3rd person plural past form 'they were' (used here to state existence/presence: 'there were/they were present').
segments.s652-g88: This clause gives a correlative alternative: اگر ... ار ... بدند functions like 'whether ... or ...'. اگر is 'if/whether', جاثلیق is the title 'Catholicos' (senior cleric), ار is the complementary 'or/if', سکوبا is an obscure/rare term here (probably 'sekuba', maybe a low court office such as cupbearer), and بدند repeats the past plural 'they were' — 'whether Catholicos or sekuba, they were [present].'
segments.s652-g185: وزان is the archaic compound (و + زان) meaning “and from that / and those.” کس که introduces a relative clause “who/that,” with the prepositional phrase با او (“with him”) marking accompaniment. شدند is the past plural of شدن “to become / to join” and here means “joined / became (an assembly),” and انجمن is the complement “assembly/meeting” placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s652-g186: همه is the subject “all/everyone.” زار and خوارند form a coordinated predicate: زار is an adjectival predicate “wretched” (not the verb “lament”), و = “and,” and خوارند is خوار + -ند, the present/plural copula meaning “are despised/lowly.” بر چشم من is a prepositional phrase “in my eyes/in my sight” modifying the whole predicate.
segments.s652-g187: وزان again = “and from that.” نامه is the noun “letter.” کز is the relative/prepositional contraction of که + از meaning “which/from which / that from,” and قیصر آمد is a simple past clause “came from Caesar.” بدوی here is an adverbial/archaic element; its exact nuance in classical usage is adverbial (roughly “at once / immediately” in context), not the senses listed in the lemma choices.
segments.s652-g188: همی is a narrative adverb (here best read as “then/straightaway”); آب تیره is the noun phrase “dark/teeming water” with adjective تیره following the noun. درآمد is the past verb “entered / flowed in” (verb of motion), and به جوی is the prepositional complement “into the stream/channel.” Note the verb follows the subject phrase, standard in Persian narrative.
segments.s652-g189: ازان کو is an archaic relative phrase “of those who / from those who.” هم‑آواز and هم‌کیش are coordinated predicates: هم‑آواز literally “sharing the same voice/like-minded/companion” and هم‌کیش “coreligionist.” اوست is the copular clause “is he/they are” completing the description of those people.
segments.s652-g190: گمانند is the present/plural verb “they suppose/they imagine.” The following predicate clause قیصر بتن خویش اوست is the content of that supposition: قیصر (Caesar) + بتن خویش literally “in his own body/in his very person” (بتن = ‘in the body/person’ + خویش reflexive ‘his own’) and اوست is the copula linking that description to the implied person — i.e. ‘they imagine him to be Caesar himself.’
segments.s652-g191: کسی را که is an idiomatic relative phrase meaning “one who / someone who.” کوتاه باشد خرد is a descriptive clause: کوتاه “short/limited” modifies خرد “intellect/wisdom,” and باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن used in relative description (“is / may be / has”), so the whole means “someone whose wisdom is short” (i.e. someone of little understanding).
segments.s652-g192: بدین here is the contracted prepositional phrase به + این “to these / toward these.” نیاکان خود is the object “these ancestors of his” (خود = reflexive/possessive “his own”), and ننگرد is the negative subjunctive/present form (ن‑ + گری/گرد stem) meaning “does not/will not look upon / will not regard.” Together: “will not regard these his ancestors.”
segments.s652-g33: This clause describes the result of an action in compact, archaic diction. بسی (here an intensive/multiplicative adverb) modifies بسته (‘bound/fastened’). The sequence بسته و پر گزندان is a coordinate phrase: 'bound and (made) full of wounds' — پر is an adjectival premodifier 'full (of)'; گزندان (plural/collective) = 'injuries/harm'. The final بدند is an old third-person plural verbal ending placed after the coordinated predicate; here it functions as the past plural verb governing the whole clause.
segments.s652-g34: برین بهره is a frozen prepositional/phrasal unit meaning 'for this share / as this portion' (بر + این → 'upon/for this'). با او ('with him') attaches to indicate accompaniment or the person affected. به زندان بدند is the result clause: به + noun indicating motion/placement 'into prison', and the verb (بدند) is the past plural 'they put/placed' (classical phrasing). Together: 'they put him into prison as that share.'
segments.s652-g35: بدان گه که is a temporal phrase 'at that time when' (بدان = به + آن, here fused; گه = 'time/occasion'), followed by باز آمد 'returned' and از روم 'from Rome'. شاه (king) is the subject. Word order is classical Persian: temporal clause → verb → adjuncts → subject.
segments.s652-g36: بنالید is a past tense verb 'he lamented/moaned'. زان ('from that' / 'thereof') introduces the object: جنبش و رنج راه — a coordinated object, 'the commotion/movement and the hardship of the road'. Note the noun-noun compound رنج راه 'hardship of the road'.
segments.s652-g37: چنان شد ز سستی که is a prototypical result clause: 'it became such from weakness that…' or 'he became so weak that…'. شد is past 'became', ز سستی gives the cause 'because of weakness', and که introduces the ensuing result clause 'that از تن بماند' — 'that (what) remained from the body' i.e. that vitality left the body. بماند is a past/stative verb 'remained/was left' describing the resulting state.
segments.s652-g38: ز ناتندرستی باردن بماند: z + noun gives cause ('from ill-health'), followed by باردن used in a semi-figurative sense 'to befall / to fall upon' (literally 'to rain'), and the stative/result verb بماند 'remained'. The line says that because of ill health something continued to afflict him — the verbal sequence describes an affliction befalling and persisting.
segments.s652-g39: This short clause reports a messenger/action: کسی 'someone' is the subject and برد is the verbal head in classical narrative ('brought/took'), followed by زی نوش‌زاد آگهی 'news concerning the newborn' — زی here = 'of/about', نوش‌زاد 'newborn' modifies آگهی 'news/report'. The clause is elliptical and typical of epic reportage.
segments.s652-g40: که introduces a reported content clause: 'that تیره شد آن فر شاهنشهی' — تیره شد 'became dark/ominous' (tireh shod), آن فر 'that farr' (the royal/divine glory), شاهنشهی 'of kingship/imperial rule' is a genitive qualifier. Overall: 'that the royal glory of the kingship turned dark/ill-omened.'
