segments.s652-g145: گروهی is an indefinite plural noun meaning 'some/a group (of people)'; که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause; یارند is a present-tense, poetic/copular phrase meaning 'are companions' (یار used as predicate, -ند marking 3rd plural); با نوش‌زاد is a prepositional phrase 'with Nushzād' (نوش‌زاد here a proper name). Together the segment says 'some who are companions with Nushzād.'
segments.s652-g146: که begins a second relative clause 'who'; جز ... ندارند یاد is a negative construction where ندارند (they do not have) governs یاد (remembrance) placed after the verb for poetic word order; جز مرگ کسری literally 'except the death of Khosrow' (جز = 'except', مرگ = 'death', کسری = the king Khosrow). The whole clause means 'who have no memory except Khosrow's death.'
segments.s652-g147: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; خود is an emphatic/reflexive 'yourself' or 'you yourself'; گذر 'passage/escape' is used nominally with یابی (verb) so together گذر یابی = 'you find/attain passage/you get through'; از روز بد is a prepositional phrase 'out of an ill day' meaning 'escape a bad day'. The verb یابی is present/subjunctive-like here as the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s652-g148: به مرگ کسی marks the occasion/cause 'at someone's death' (به = 'at/toward'); شاد باشی is a subordinate subjunctive 'that you be glad' (شاد adjective + باشی subjunctive of 'to be'); سزد is an impersonal/3rd-person-present 'it befits/it is fitting'. The structure is: 'If ... , it is fitting that you be glad at someone's death.'
segments.s652-g149: و دیگر opens another point 'and another/moreover'; که introduces the clause; از مرگ شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/concerning the death of kings'; داد is the noun 'justice' placed at the end of the phrase as the theme/topic. The segment sets up the theme 'as for the death of kings, (regarding) justice...'.
segments.s652-g150: نگیرد is a negative present-subjunctive/indicative form of گرفتن meaning 'does not obtain/take' (here used impersonally to mean 'no one obtains'); کسی follows as an indefinite 'anyone/no one' under the negation; یاد is the object 'remembrance/memory'; جز بدنژاد is the exception phrase 'except the ignoble-born'. The word order is poetic: verb-negation + indefinite + object + exception, yielding 'no one gains remembrance (or justice) except the ignoble-born.'
segments.s652-g151: سر نوش‌زاد is the subject 'the head of Nushzād' (سر = head, here figurative for mind); از خرد is a prepositional phrase 'from wisdom/reason'; بازگشت is a past-tense intransitive verb 'turned back/returned' meaning idiomatically 'lost his reason' or 'his mind left him'. The line says 'Nushzād's head/brain left wisdom.'
segments.s652-g152: چنین 'thus/so' links the result; دیو is the subject 'a demon'; با او 'with him'; هم‌آواز is a compound adjective/adverb meaning 'in unison/like-minded' or 'an ally'; گشت is the past-tense of گشتن meaning 'became/turned into' (here 'became [of one mind]'). Together: 'thus a demon became allied/one-minded with him.'
segments.s652-g153: This is an exclamatory / quoted line: نباشد is a negative jussive/imperative-like form meaning 'let there not be' or 'may it not be'; برو is the imperative 'go' (vocative/command inside the quote). پایدار modifies سخن that follows, so the phrase literally strings together a negative wish + imperative + adjective + demonstrative + noun: 'Let this speech not be lasting — go.'
segments.s652-g154: برافراخت is a past simple verb 'he raised/erected/started' (here narrational past). چون introduces a temporal clause 'as/when'; خواست is past 'he wished/desired'; آمد is past 'he came'; ببن is an imperative/infinitival root meaning 'see/look' used here to mark purpose or result ('came to see'). The clause reads: 'he raised [himself/it], and when he wished, he came to look.'
segments.s652-g155: نبایست is an older modal meaning 'ought not / it should not be'; کو here functions as a relative/indefinite 'who/that' (archaic usage) referring to 'anyone'; نزد ما = 'in our presence / before us'; دستگاه is the noun 'court / establishment'. The whole segment states the obligation/ban: 'there should not be anyone who, in our presence, establishes a court.'
segments.s652-g156: بدین آگهی is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this news' (بدین = به + دین/this in poetic fusion). خیره کردی is a two-word verbal sequence: خیره (stunned/staring) + کردی (you did — 2nd person past), i.e. 'you caused (them) to stare' or 'you stared/was stunned'; تباه is a predicative/adjectival result 'ruined/ ruined it'. Grammatically this ties as the result of the preceding clause: 'and by this news you made [them] stare and were ruined/caused ruin.'
segments.s652-g157: اگر is the conditional 'if'; تخت is 'throne'; گشتی here is a past/result-state (from گشتن) meaning 'became/was left' (poetic past usage); ز indicates separation 'of/from'; خسرو is the sovereign's name/title; تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty'. The clause means 'if the throne was emptied of Khosrow'.
segments.s652-g158: همو is emphatic 'that very one / he himself' used as subject, بود is the past copula 'was'; زیبای is an adjectival/nominal form 'the beauty/ornament of' and شاهنشهی ('شاهنشاهی' in modern spelling) means 'kingship / imperial rule'. Word order places the emphatic pronoun before the predicate: 'he himself was the beauty (ornament) of kingship.'
segments.s652-g159: چنین بود is a fixed narrative phrase 'thus was' or 'such was he'; خود is emphatic 'himself'; در خور here functions as the compound درخور meaning 'worthy/appropriate to'; کیش = 'creed/custom/character'; اوی is an enclitic pronoun 'he/his' referring back to the subject. Grammatically: 'Thus he himself was in accordance with his creed' (i.e. outwardly fitting to his faith/custom).
segments.s652-g160: سزاوار is 'deserving / fitting'; جان here is 'life' (often used idiomatically); بداندیش is a compound adjective meaning 'ill‑minded / malicious'; اوی is the third‑person pronoun 'he'. The sequence reads as a characterizing predicate: '[he was] deserving (worthy) in life / or fit for life; [nevertheless] he was ill‑minded' — the line juxtaposes external worthiness with inner ill will.
segments.s652-g161: ازین بر is an adverbial phrase meaning “besides this / from this on” (here از + این). The structure is literal: بر دل «on the heart» followed by the two abstract nouns اندیشه و باک “thought and fear” and the negative copula نیست “is not” to state absence. Word order: adverbial (ازین بر) → locative (بر دل) → items (اندیشه و باک) → predicate (نیست).
segments.s652-g162: اگر introduces a conditional clause. کیش فرزند ما is a genitive/possessive sequence 'the faith/creed of our child' (کیش + فرزندِ ما), followed by the predicate پاک نیست ‘is not pure’. The negative copula نیست marks the condition's content as negative.
segments.s652-g163: وزین کس is here an older/clitic combination meaning roughly 'and those (people)' (و + زِ/زین); the following relative clause که با او بهم ساختند 'who joined/combined with him' modifies کس. ساختند is past plural 'they made/formed' used idiomatically with بهم to mean 'they joined/formed an alliance'.
segments.s652-g164: وز here is the conjunctive + prepositional fusion (و + زِ) meaning 'and from/of'; آزرم appears to be a proper name (آزرمِ ما = 'our Azarm'), followed by دل بپرداختند, an idiom 'they paid/gave their heart' (دل + پرداختند) meaning 'they devoted/turned their hearts (to)'. The verb پرداخـتند is past plural.
segments.s652-g165: وزان = و + زِ + ان, 'and from that / and of that'. خواسته here functions as 'that which was sought / the desire (which...)' (a deverbal/nominal use). کو is an archaic/poetic relative particle (roughly 'which/that' or 'who'), and تبه کرد means 'committed evil / did wickedly' (تبه used adjectivally/nominatively + کرد 'did'). نیز 'also/likewise' links this unit to the previous remark.
segments.s652-g166: همی is an emphatic/incessive particle here functioning as an intensifier ('indeed/at all'); بر دل ما places the feeling 'on our heart'; نسنجد is a negative verb phrase here meaning 'does not sit well / is not pleasing' (archaic usage), and به چیز 'by/for anything' means 'in any respect'. The clause thus denies that such a desire is acceptable to the speaker's heart in any way.
segments.s652-g167: A nominal predicate with a plural ending: three coordinated adjectives describe the people—بداندیش 'ill‑minded/malicious', بیکار 'idle/useless', and بدگوهرند 'they are of bad/metaphorical jewel—i.e. base character'. The final -ند is the 3rd-person plural copula.
segments.s652-g168: بدین is a fused prepositional phrase 'with/for this' (به + این); زیردستی here means 'underling(s) / those in subordination'; نه ... خورند is a negative construction 'they do not خورند' where خورند in this idiom means 'fit / suit / belong' (archaic sense). Literally 'such underlings do not fit into this' → 'are not acceptable/fit for this.'
segments.s652-g265: 'جزان' is the poetic contraction of 'جز آن' introducing an exception/clause ('except that' / 'anyone who'); 'هرک' = 'هر که' = 'whoever'. Word order places the object 'ما را' (us + object marker 'را') before the locative phrase 'به دل' ('in his heart') and the predicate 'دشمنست' (old form of 'است' with '-ست'), so the clause reads literally 'whoever (هرک) (marks) us (ما را) in heart (به دل) is enemy (دشمنست)'. 'دشمنست' is a present-state predicate 'is hostile / is an enemy'.
segments.s652-g266: The preposition 'ز' (poetic for 'from/of') introduces origin: 'from the seed of cruelty'. 'تخم' = 'seed' followed by the genitive/qualifying noun 'جفا' = 'cruelty, injustice'. 'پیشه' ('profession/practice') here is appositional/qualifying the following predicate, and 'آهرمنست' (Ahriman + old copula '-ست') states identity/affiliation: 'is of Ahriman' (i.e. Ahrimanic, belonging to Ahriman).
segments.s652-g267: Here 'ز' = 'from/of' marks source: 'from us' + noun 'نیکوییها' ('good deeds/virtues') — the poetic word order places 'ما' before the noun. The verb 'نگیرند' is present negative plural of 'گرفتن' ('do not take') used idiomatically with 'یاد' to mean 'do not keep/retain in memory' = 'they do not remember'. 'یاد' = 'remembrance' is the thing not taken/kept.
segments.s652-g268: Syntactically 'تو را آزمایش' is a nominal phrase meaning 'the testing/ordeal of you' (object-marked 'تو را' marks 'you' as the one tested). 'بس' is postpositive 'enough'. 'ازنوش زاد' is a compact poetic clause: 'ازنو' = 'again' precedes the verb 'زاد' (here an archaic/past-form, 'was born' / 'came into being'), so 'was born again' — the subject is omitted and recovered from context (third person).
segments.s652-g269: 'ز نظاره' = 'from the sight' (i.e. 'at the sight/of seeing'); the preposition + noun introduces a circumstantial clause. 'هرکس که' = 'whoever / anyone who' introduces the relative clause; 'دشنام داد' is a simple past: 'gave an insult' = 'uttered an insult/insulted'. The whole segment functions as 'when/at the sight of anyone who insulted'.
segments.s652-g270: 'زبانش' = 'his tongue' (possessive clitic '-ش'). 'بجنبید' is a past/perfective event 'moved / was stirred' (here indicating a vocal or physical reaction). The phrase 'بر نوش زاد' combines 'بر' (toward/on), 'نوش' (the drink/cup) and the verb 'زاد' (here again an archaic past verb 'arose/sprung' or 'came into being/moved toward'); together the clause describes the ensuing action: 'his tongue moved and he sprang toward the drink' — the subject is the same omitted third person as before. Poetic compression allows the verb 'زاد' to describe the sudden movement toward the drink.
segments.s652-g271: 'بران' = 'on that / thereupon' (anaphoric/adverbial), 'ویژه' = 'special/especially'. 'دشنام ما' (word order inverted poetically) = 'insults aimed at us' (with 'ما' marking 'us' as the target). 'خواستند' is past plural 'they wanted/desired' — the phrase therefore means 'they particularly wished to hurl insults at us.'
segments.s652-g272: 'به هنگام' is a fixed locative phrase 'at the time of'; it governs the verbal noun 'بدگفتن' ('speaking ill / slandering'). 'آراستند' is past plural of 'آراستن' meaning 'they arranged / prepared / set up' — here it says that at the time for bad-speaking they made ready (prepared themselves to speak ill). The clause describes the timing and their deliberate preparation.
segments.s652-g201: گرفتنش (گرفتن + enclitic -ش) is an infinitive with the attached object pronoun “him/it”; better is expressed by the comparative adjective بهتر following the infinitive. ز here is the poetic preposition ‘than’ introducing the thing being compared (کشتن). بود is the past copula (‘was’) linking the clause into a past-tense judgement: “Taking him was better than killing.”
segments.s652-g202: مگرش = مگر + enclitic -ش: an exception/conditional particle with the pronoun, ‘unless he…’. The rest is an infinitival clause از گنه بازگشتن with بود as the copula — literally “unless there was his returning from sin,” i.e. “unless he repented / turned away from sin.” This is an archaic/poetic way to express the conditional exception.
segments.s652-g203: از آبی introduces the source “from water.” کزو is the relative adverb ‘from which,’ linking that source to the following clause. سرو آزاد is the noun + postpositive adjective ‘cypress (that is) noble/free,’ and رست is the past form ‘sprang/grew.’ Poetic word order places the relative and adjectives around the verb.
segments.s652-g204: سزدگر here functions as an impersonal evaluative ‘it would be fitting’ (or ‘if fitting’). نباید is the impersonal negative modal ‘one should not,’ followed by the dative بدو ‘to it/him’ and the phrase خاک شست ‘wash soil’ (شست is a past-form used poetically after the modal). Together: ‘it would not be fitting that anyone wash soil upon it.’
segments.s652-g205: وگر is the conditional ‘if.’ خوار is the subject adjective ‘despised/lowly (one).’ گیرد is a present/habitual third-person form ‘takes/seizes’ used for the hypothetical. تن ارجمند = ‘an honorable body/person,’ with ارجمند postpositive describing تن (the person).
segments.s652-g206: به پستی is a goal/result phrase ‘into baseness/low estate.’ نهد (from نهاد) is the present-tense verb ‘places/puts’ used here in the sense ‘renders’ or ‘brings down.’ روی سرو بلند = ‘the face/appearance of the tall (lofty) cypress’ (روی = face/countenance; سرو بلند = lofty cypress) is the object being put into lowliness.
segments.s652-g207: سرش is ‘his head’ with the enclitic pronoun. برگراید is a poetic/potential form meaning ‘turns’ or ‘is inclined to turn’ (برگشتن-derived, here ‘turn away/aside’). ز بالین ناز = ‘from the pillow of fondness/indulgence’ (ز = from; بالین = pillow; ناز = tenderness/indulgence), indicating what he turns away from.
segments.s652-g208: This line is compact and uses archaic/poetic forms: مدار is used imperatively/negatively here (archaic usage) meaning roughly ‘do not remove/withdraw’; ایچ is an emphatic negation ‘at all’; ازو = ‘from him’; گرز و شمشیر = ‘mace and sword’ (the instruments); باز is a short particle meaning ‘away/back’ or marks the instruments as being taken away. The whole gives the prohibition: ‘by no means take the mace and sword from him.’
segments.s652-g473: اگر is the conditional particle 'if'. در دلت is a locative phrase: در 'in' + دل 'heart' + -ت the 2nd-person possessive suffix = 'in your heart'. هیچ is a quantifier here meaning 'any' (poetic/older usage) placed before the noun حب ('love'). علیست should be read as علی است ('is Ali' / 'is of Ali') where the copula است is enclitic -ست attached in poetry; the whole phrase means 'if in your heart there is any love of Ali.' Word order is slightly inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s652-g474: تو را puts 'you' in the role of beneficiary/affected party: تو + object/emphatic marker را = 'for you' or 'you (as object)'. روز محشر is a temporal noun phrase 'the Day of Resurrection/Judgement' placed before the predicate (no preposition needed in classical style). به خواهش uses به to mark means/instrument 'by/through' and خواهش 'pleading/request' (here: pleading on someone's behalf). ولیست is ولی است, with the copula attached as -ست; ولی here means 'a wali/intercessor/saint' and refers back to ʿAli. In conditional clauses the present copula expresses the result that will obtain (i.e. he will intercede for you).
segments.s652-g225: نه is the negation applying to the whole clause. آسانیی دید is a poetic word order where دید (saw/experienced) follows the object آسانیی (ease) — literally 'saw ease' = 'experienced ease'. بی‌رنج کس is a restrictive phrase 'a person without suffering'; placed after the verb for poetic inversion: the sense is 'no one without toil experiences ease.'
segments.s652-g226: که introduces a clause giving the reason or qualification. روشن زمانه is a nominal phrase 'the age is bright/illuminated.' برینست is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'is for the high/exalted' (برین + است contracted), and و بس is a short concluding particle: 'and that is enough' / 'and nothing more.'
segments.s652-g227: تو is the emphatic subject 'you'; با چرخ گردان is a prepositional phrase 'with the turning wheel' where چرخ گردان (wheel + turning) is a stock metaphor for the revolving heavens or fickle fate. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do' and دوستی is the object 'friendship' (i.e. 'do not befriend').
segments.s652-g228: که‌گه (که گه) = 'for sometimes' / 'at times.' مغز اویی literally reads 'morgz + او + یی' and here means 'it has substance/core' (مغز = core). اویی is the pronoun form referring back to the wheel/fate. گه = 'sometimes' again, and پوستی 'of the skin' or 'superficial' — the contrast 'sometimes core, sometimes only skin' expresses inconstancy.
segments.s652-g229: چه جویی is an interrogative 'what (do you) seek?' with جویی (from جست‌وجو) used as 'seeking.' زکردار او = 'from his deeds' (ز = from/of + کردار = deed). رنگ و بوی 'color and scent' here stand for outward appearance and attractive qualities (i.e. reputation or show).
segments.s652-g230: بخواهد is a third-person present/future-like form 'he will want / he desires' and takes the following infinitive ربودن 'to snatch/steal' as its object. چو here functions temporally as 'when,' and به نمود روی = 'at the appearance of his face' (به نمود = 'in/at appearance', روی = 'face' or outward aspect). Altogether: 'he will want to snatch them when his face appears.'
segments.s652-g231: بدان گه is an adverbial phrase 'at that time' (بدان is an older fusion of به + آن). بود is the past-tense copula 'was.' بیم رنج و گزند = 'fear (bīm) of suffering (ranj) and harm (gazand)' — a noun phrase functioning as the subject complement: 'there was fear of suffering and injury.'
segments.s652-g232: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for / because.' گردون گردان is a doubled epithet for the heavens/fate ('the turning sky' or 'revolving fortunes'), where گردون = the sky/fate and گردان = revolving. برآرد is a present/future-form verb 'brings up / may raise up,' and بلند is the adverb/adjective 'high' — 'for the turning heaven may lift [one] high.'
segments.s652-g241: نه negates the whole clause that follows. The noun phrase 'پروای رای مسیحا بود' is built with 'پروای' (concern) + 'رای' (counsel/opinion) and the copula 'بود' (was) attached; the sense is that there was no concern for the Messiah's opinion. Note that the token 'مسیحابود' bundles 'مسیحا' (Messiah, genitive) with the copula 'بود' in the data, though here 'مسیحا' functions as the possessor of 'رای'.
segments.s652-g242: This is a result clause introduced by the preposition 'به' (to/at, here marking outcome): 'به فرجام خصمش' = 'at the end/ outcome of his enmity'. 'چلیپا بود' is a simple past copular clause meaning 'was a cross' (i.e. ended in crucifixion). 'بود' is the past copula.
segments.s652-g243: 'دگر' means 'other/else' and begins the clause; 'هرکه هست' = 'whoever is' (present copula 'هست'). 'از' marks membership or origin ('from/among'), so 'از پراگندگان' = 'from/among the spreaders/disseminators'. Word order is subject ('دگر هرکه هست') followed by the prepositional/partitive phrase.
segments.s652-g244: A coordinated list of attributes/groups: 'بدآموز' is an adjective/noun meaning 'corrupting/ill‑instructive', then 'و بدخواه' ('and ill‑wisher/malevolent'), followed by another 'و' and 'از بندگان' ('from/among the servants'). 'از' again marks membership; the whole phrase further specifies the people just mentioned.
segments.s652-g245: 'از ایشان' = 'from/among them'. 'یکی' here = 'any one' (indefinite/one of them). 'برتری رای' is a nominal phrase 'superiority of opinion/counsel.' 'نیست' is the negative copula 'is not/there is not', so the clause states that no one among them possesses superior counsel.
segments.s652-g246: 'دم باد' is an idiomatic noun phrase ('a puff/blast of wind') formed by two nouns; it functions as the subject. 'با رای ایشان' = 'with their counsel' (instrumental/associative), and 'یکیست' (archaic present copula) means 'is one/identical' — i.e. 'a puff of wind is the same as their counsel' (their counsel amounts to nothing).
segments.s652-g247: 'به جنگ' = 'to/into war' (direction/setting). 'ار' is an archaic form of 'اگر' meaning 'if'. 'گرفته شود' combines the past participle 'گرفته' (taken/seized) with 'شود' (subjunctive/passive auxiliary) to yield the conditional/passive 'is/should be taken up'. 'نوش‌زاد' (literally 'newly born') is a vocative address here — the addressee of the following imperative.
segments.s652-g248: 'برو' is the imperative 'go'. 'زین' is an archaic contracted prepositional form (≈ 'از این' = 'of/from these'); in the dataset the listed senses are literal 'saddle' meanings, but here it functions as 'of/from these'. 'سخنها' = 'these words/speeches', 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not', 'هیچ' intensifies to 'at all/anything', and 'یاد' functions as 'mention/remember' (i.e. 'do not mention any of these things').
segments.s652-g297: بدانگه که = a temporal phrase meaning 'at that moment' followed by the conjunction که 'when/that'. خیزد is a 3rd‑person singular present (classical/narrative) of خاست/خیزیدن 'to rise, arise' and stands before its subject for poetic inversion. خروش خروس is the subject phrase 'the rooster's cry' (noun + genitive), occupying post‑verbal position for rhythm.
segments.s652-g298: ز درگاه = 'from the court/threshold' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and درگاه denotes the royal court/presence. برخاست is the past of برخاستن 'arose/rose up', here a simple past narrative verb. آوای کوس = 'the sound/voice of the drum' (آوا = sound, کوس = war‑drum), a genitive phrase that is the subject of برخاست.
segments.s652-g299: سپاهی بزرگ = 'a great army' (noun + adjective). از مداین برفت is a past narrative clause: از + place name مداین 'from Madā'in' and برفت is an archaic/poetic past form of رفتن 'went/departed' (note: the supplied senses list for برفت is incorrect; here it functions as the verb 'departed').
segments.s652-g300: This clause is compact and somewhat ambiguous: بشد is the classical past/auxiliary sense 'it happened/it became'; رام برزین is presented as a unit — likely a personal name/epithet (Ram Barzin) rather than the adjective 'tame' + name; سوی جنگ تفت = 'toward the battle at (or of) Taft' with سوی marking direction. Grammatically, بشد governs the following nominal phrase (subject/name) and the directional phrase.
segments.s652-g301: پس = 'then' (temporal/conclusive adverb). آگاهی آمد is an idiom 'information/news came' where آگاهی functions as a noun 'news/notice' and آمد is the past 'came'. سوی نوش‌زاد = 'to Nowshzad' with سوی marking direction/recipient; نوش‌زاد here is a proper name (a prince), not the literal 'newborn'.
segments.s652-g302: سپاه انجمن کرد is a light-verb construction: سپاه (the army) + انجمن 'assembly/gathering' + کرد 'made/did' = 'he gathered the army' or 'the army assembled (he caused an assembly)'. و links the next clause. روزی بداد is idiomatic: روزی ('provisions, livelihood' in context) and بداد is an archaic/periphrastic past meaning 'gave' (here 'he gave them sustenance'); note the supplied senses for روزی/بداد in the word list do not match this classical usage, so those entries are irregular.
segments.s652-g303: همه جاثلیقان = 'all the catholicoi' (همه quantifies the following plural noun). و به طریق روم = 'and in the manner/way of Rome' where به طریق marks 'in the way of' and روم = Byzantium/Romans; this phrase modifies or parallels the preceding group.
segments.s652-g304: که بودند = a relative clause 'who were' (که introduces the relative; بودند is the past plural of بودن). زان مرز آبادبوم = 'from that borderland' where زان = 'from that', مرز = 'border', and آبادبوم = 'prosperous/native dwelling/land'; the whole clause specifies those mentioned before as hailing from that frontier region.
segments.s652-g441: کنون (adverb) is fronted for emphasis: 'now'. جان او is the subject ('his soul'); با + مسیحا is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase ('with the Messiah'). یکیست is a copular predicate ('is one/is united'), the fused form یکی + است typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s652-g442: همانست functions as a demonstrative + copula ('that is the case' / 'such is it'), followed by a 'ک' clause (کاین) introducing the content: 'that this ...'. خسته is a nominalized adjective here ('the weary one' = the corpse). بردار is used as 'bearer/carrier' (noun) and نیست negates existence ('there is no carrier').
segments.s652-g443: مسیحی is the noun-subject ('a Christian'). بشهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the inner part of the city' (بـ = in/to + شهر + اندرون = inner). هرک + بود ('whoever he was/there was') gives a generalizing sense; بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s652-g444: نبد is an older negative past form (variant of نبود) meaning 'there was not'. هیچ quantifies the negation ('not a single/none'). ترسای is 'a pious/devout person'. رخ ('face') here is modified by ناشخود ('involuntarily/not composed'), so the clause states that there wasn't a single devout person whose face was not involuntarily moved — i.e. all showed emotion.
segments.s652-g445: خروش آمد is a simple verbal phrase 'a cry/uproar came/rose'. از indicates source ('from Shahruz' — Shahruz is a proper name). مرد و زن lists the people affected/raising the cry ('men and women').
segments.s652-g446: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/so that'). بودند is past plural 'they were'. یک سر is an idiomatic phrase (literally 'one head') meaning 'all together/at once'; شدند is 'became' and انجمن is 'an assembly' — together: they became one assembled group.
segments.s652-g447: تن شهریار places تن ('body') before the title to mean 'the body of the king' or 'the person of the king'. دلیر و جوان are adjectival attributes following the noun, 'brave and young'. This is a poetic appositive description.
segments.s652-g448: دل و دیده are two nouns in a parallel (heart and eye). شاه نوشین‌روان is the noun with an epithet: شاه 'the king' qualified by نوشین‌روان ('sweet‑souled' / pleasant‑minded). The phrase lists the beloved inner qualities of the king.
segments.s652-g377: سوی = 'toward' (directional preposition) comes first, then پاک یزدان 'the Pure God' as its object. شد is the past/resultative 'became/turned' and attaches to آن رای پاک ('that pure counsel') which follows آن (demonstrative) + رای (counsel/plan) + پاک (adjective). Word order is Topic — Verb — Subject-Phrase in this poetic line: literally 'Toward the Pure God became that pure counsel.'
segments.s652-g378: بلندی ندید is a negated perception clause: بلندی 'height/exaltation' (the thing expected) + ندید 'was not seen' (past of دیدن used impersonally). اندرین تیره خاک 'in this dark/bleak earth' is a locative/locutional phrase (اندرین = 'in this', تیره 'dark/bleak', خاک 'earth') modifying the whole clause. The clause thus means 'no loftiness/exaltation was seen in this dark earth.'
segments.s652-g379: اگرمن 'if I' is an archaic fused conditional particle + pronoun; شوم is subjunctive/potential 'I should become/be (killed)' and is followed by کشته (past participle 'killed') forming 'if I should be killed'. زان 'from/for that' + باک نیست 'there is no fear' — باک (fear) is negated by نیست. The sequence gives a conditional reassurance: 'If I am killed, there is no fear from that.'
segments.s652-g380: کجا here functions rhetorically as 'for/where' introducing a justificatory clause. زهر مرگست = 'is the poison of death' (زهر 'poison' + مرگست 'is death/it is death'), and و تریاک نیست links as a contrastive continuation: 'and there is no opium' — i.e. where death is the poison, even opium (a drug/cure) is absent. The clauses are juxtaposed to justify the speaker's lack of fear.
segments.s652-g381: بگفت is simple past 'he said'; این سخن 'these words' is the object; پیش + پیروز پیر 'before the victorious elder' places the speech in the elder's presence. Order is Verb — Object — prepositional phrase giving the scene: 'He said these words before the victorious elder.'
segments.s652-g382: بپوشید is past 'he covered' (poetic simple past). روی هوا را is the direct object with روی 'face/surface' and هوا 'air/sky' marked by را (definite object marker). بتیر is an instrumental/qualifier meaning 'with arrows' or 'by arrow(s)'. The clause describes an active covering of the sky's surface by arrows: 'he covered the face of the air with arrows.'
segments.s652-g383: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they departed/advanced'. گردان لشکر is a genitive/construct phrase 'divisions (گردان) of the army (لشکر)'; ز جای 'from their place/position' uses poetic ز for 'from'. The line describes troop units breaking camp and moving off: 'The army's divisions departed from their place.'
segments.s652-g384: خروش آمد is 'a roar/clamor came' (خروش 'uproar/roar' + آمد 'came'). از کوس وز کرنای gives the sources: از کوس 'from the war-drum' and وز کرنای (poetic compression of و ز کرنای) 'and from the trumpet' — here وز is functioning as the connective + preposition cluster. The whole means 'a roar came from the drum and the trumpet.'
segments.s652-g289: چو here is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. The phrase از ره سوی رام is a prepositional/directional phrase: از (from/on) + ره (road/way) + سوی (toward) + رام (a place-name here). Word order is literary: directional phrase comes before the subject برزین, and the verb رسید (past, 3rd sing.) ends the clause — a common SOV/fluid order in epic Persian.
segments.s652-g290: بگفت is a literary past-tense verb 'he said/related' (ب- here is the archaic verbal prefix found in classical Persian forms). آنچ is a fused relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which / what'. از introduces the source 'from King Khosrow' and شنید is simple past 'he heard'. The clause therefore reports the content Barzīn spoke: what he had heard from Khosrow.
segments.s652-g291: چو آن گفته شد is a temporal clause 'when that was said'. گفته is a past participle/predicate 'said', and شد functions as the auxiliary 'was' (passive/compound past). نامه او (his letter) is the following noun phrase; بداد here appears in the text as the verb meaning 'gave/handed over' but the provided lemma senses do not list that verbal reading, so the listed sense does not match the usage in context.
segments.s652-g292: به فرمان introduces an instrument/condition 'by order/decree'. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that' and فرمود is the reporting/command verb 'he commanded/issued'. با marks accompaniment 'with' and نوش زاد is a short noun phrase 'drink (نوش) and provisions/food (زاد)'; زاد here is used in the sense 'provisions' rather than 'birth'.
segments.s652-g293: This segment lists two infinitival/nominalized actions: سپه کردن ('to make/muster an army') uses the light verb کردن after سپه; و joins the next phrase; جنگ را ساختن ('to make/prepare war') uses را as the definite/direct-object marker on جنگ and ساختن 'to build/make' as the verb. Both constructions are infinitival/nominal in sense (describing intended actions).
segments.s652-g294: وز here is an archaic fused form (classical poetry often writes ו + ز as وز) introducing 'and from/of', followed by آزرم او (a proper name + the pronoun او placed after the name, a classical genitive/identifying construction 'Azarm of him / his Azarm'). مغز پرداختن is an infinitival verb phrase: مغز 'brain/core' + پرداختن; in epic usage پرداختن can mean 'to take away/dispatch/bring about' (here idiomatically 'to remove/kill, take the brain'). The provided modern senses do not fully cover these classical/poetic uses, so some listed lemmas do not match directly.
segments.s652-g295: چوآن is the fused classical form چو آن 'when that'; نامه is the object 'the letter'. برخواند is a past tense verb meaning 'read aloud / recited' (classical recitative verb), and مرد کهن 'the old man' is the agent/subject. Poetic word order places the verb before the subject here.
segments.s652-g296: شنید is past tense 'he heard'. از فرستاده marks the source/agent 'from the messenger'. چندی سخن is a noun phrase 'some/a few words' (چندی modifies سخن); the whole clause reports that the old man heard a few words from the messenger.
segments.s652-g417: نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor.' Here the predicate is expressed with the impersonal/necessitative باید after the noun (کافور باید = 'camphor is needed'). The second item in the list follows and with و links مشک and عبیر as additional perfumes; the whole clause means 'neither camphor nor musk and ambergris (are needed).'
segments.s652-g418: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/since.' زین is a poetic contraction (زِ این) meaning 'from this' (poetic usage), followed by جهان 'the world/this life.' کشته گشتم is a passive/perfect-like expression: past participle کشته + گشتم (گشتن used here as auxiliary) = 'I was killed.' بتیر ('به' + 'تیر' fused) expresses the agent/means 'by an arrow.'
segments.s652-g419: بگفت is simple past 'he said.' این refers back to the words just spoken. The conjunction و links the next action. لب را is the definite object marked with را. بهم is an adverb 'together' (here 'together/closed'), and برنهاد is a past verb 'placed/raised' used idiomatically 'brought (the lips) together' = 'pressed/closed his lips.'
segments.s652-g420: شد is a polyvalent past verb 'became/it happened' used here to state what happened next. آن is the demonstrative 'that.' The sequence نامور شیردل نوش‌زاد is a string of epithets (renowned, lion‑hearted) finishing with a proper name/epithet نوش‌زاد; together they identify the person affected in the previous clause.
segments.s652-g421: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' آگاه شد is 'became aware/informed' (آگاه as adjective/noun + شد as intransitive 'became'). لشکر is the subject 'the army.' از مرگ شاه is a prepositional/construct phrase 'about/of the king's death' (از marking the source/concern). Note the poetic inversion where the verb follows the subject.
segments.s652-g422: پراگنده is an adjectival state 'scattered.' گشتند is the past plural of گشتن 'they became' (here 'they were scattered'). زان is a fused/poetic form meaning 'from that' (زان رزمگاه = 'from that battlefield'). رزمگاه is 'battlefield.' The clause literally 'they became scattered from that battlefield.'
segments.s652-g423: چو = 'when.' بَشنید is simple past 'he heard.' کو here is an archaic/poetic relative particle 'that/which' introducing the reported clause ('that the champion was killed') — this use is not the interrogative 'where.' کشته شد is the passive/result 'was killed.' پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero.'
segments.s652-g424: This clause gives the reaction: غریوان is here a personal name/epithet (not the literal 'neckline' sense in the lexicon) and so is treated as the actor. به بالین او = 'to his bedside' (بالین literally 'pillow' used idiomatically as 'bedside'). شد + دوان is a common poetic pairing where شد 'became/was set' + دوان (run/rush) yields 'set off running' = 'ran quickly.' او is the pronoun 'him/her' referring back to the fallen.
segments.s652-g281: کسی کو is an archaic relative construction meaning “someone who…”. کو here functions like a relative pronoun (“who”) rather than the usual interrogative “where”. بجوید is the classical present (the b‑prefix + present-stem) with همی adding continuous/habitual force (“constantly/always seeks”). روزگار is the object: in this idiom it means one’s fortunes or fate (what one’s life/time brings).
segments.s652-g282: که تا introduces a result/temporal clause here equivalent to “until/so that…”. تا is the temporal/concessive “until”. سست گردد is a subordinate verbal phrase: گردد is the classical 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of گشتن used here as “become,” so “becomes weak.” تن شهریار is a genitive construction “the body (تن) of the king (شهریار).”
segments.s652-g283: به کار آورد (literally “put into work”) is an idiom meaning “to employ/ resort to” or “to bring about.” Here آورد is used in the classical (poetic) form and governs the direct objects کژی و دشمنی (“crookedness and enmity”), joined by و.
segments.s652-g284: This segment lists further nouns joined by و. بداندیشی is a noun meaning malice/bad intent. کیش is ‘sect/creed’ and is modified by the adjective آهرمنی (“Ahrimanic, demonic”), so کیش آهرمنی = “the Ahrimanic creed/faith.”
segments.s652-g285: بدین پادشاهی = ‘for/to this kingship’ (بدین is an archaic/formal prepositional phrase = به + این). نباشد (negative form of بودن) is used impersonally here with رواست to mean “it is not fitting/ proper.” رواست = روا + است, with روا “fitting/permissible” and است the copula. Together: “It is not fitting for this kingship.”
segments.s652-g286: که introduces the content clause “that …”. The following nouns are listed in apposition (فر ‘royal glory’, سر ‘head’ here as part of the regalia/honour, افسر ‘helmet’—a word for crown/helmet—and چهر ‘face/appearance’). ماست is ما + ست (copula) meaning “is ours” or “belongs to us.”
segments.s652-g287: نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن “to place/set” (they placed). برنامه is the direct object “plan.” بر here is the preposition “on/upon/under,” and مهر شاه is a genitive phrase “the king’s مهر,” with مهر here meaning favour/seal (i.e. the king’s seal or goodwill).
segments.s652-g288: فرستاده is the noun “the messenger” (or ‘the one sent’). برگشت is a past verbal noun/verb “returned/resumed.” پویان is here a proper name (the messenger’s name) in apposition to فرستاده. به راه is the adverbial prepositional phrase “on the road / on his way.” The whole means “the messenger Puyan returned / resumed his journey.”
segments.s652-g465: مگردان is a negative imperative addressed to 'you' (do not turn/withdraw); it governs the following noun phrase. سرازدین here functions as a religious/title word connected as the domain from which one must not turn; وز is an archaic connective/prepositional element linking سرازدین to راستی (here meaning 'from/and'), and راستی is the abstract noun 'righteousness/uprightness' serving as the object of مگردان.
segments.s652-g466: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/because') giving the reason. خشم خدای is a genitive phrase 'the wrath/anger of God' acting as subject; آورد is a third-person verbal form ('brings/causes'), used here in a poetic register to state the consequence; کاستی is the result noun 'lack/deficiency' (object/complement).
segments.s652-g467: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; این is the demonstrative 'this' governed by بشنوی (second-person present/subjunctive/optative form 'you hear/are told'), so چو + بشنوی = 'when you hear this'. دل زغم بازکش is an imperative clause: دل 'heart' is the thing affected, زغم 'from sorrow' is an ablative phrase, and بازکش is an imperative verb 'draw back/withdraw' directed at the heart. (Note: بازکش here is a poetic imperative 'withdraw' rather than the lexical senses given in the word list.)
segments.s652-g468: مزن is a negative imperative 'do not strike/do not strike down' used idiomatically here (= 'do not press/strike/bit[e]'). بر لبت is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against your lip' (بر = on; لبت = your lip). The second بر repeats the preposition introducing the cause phrase 'بر ز تیمار تش' where ز is the poetic 'from/because of' and تیمار تش is 'the anxious/feverish care' (تیمار 'care/treatment' + تش 'fever/ardor'). Together: 'do not press your lip out of anxious fever.'
segments.s652-g469: گرت is a poetic contraction meaning 'if (you) have/if it be that' (conditional particle); هست is the copula 'is/there is' (here supporting the existence/possession clause); جام is 'cup' used metonymically for 'wine/drink'; می‌زرد is a present-tense poetic verb form ('turns pale/goes light') used descriptively of the cup/drink; خواه here functions elliptically in the sense 'whether' (classical correlative 'خواه... یا...'), a usage not reflected in the simple lexical senses listed.
segments.s652-g470: به دل خرمی را is a phrase meaning 'for the heart's merriment' (به = for/to; دل = heart; خرمی = mirth/delight; را = object/topic marker attaching emphasis). مدان is an archaic/poetic negative imperative form meaning 'do not regard/consider' (from an old form of 'دانستن' used here in the prohibitive sense). از گناه is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from sin' giving the reason or the thing you should not make the delight into.
segments.s652-g471: نشاط وطرب جوی is an exhortation: نشاط 'joy' and وطرب 'and delight' are nouns followed by جوی (imperative/verb-stem 'seek'/'desire') forming 'seek joy and delight.' وسستی مکن places the negative imperative مکن ('do not') after وسستی ('languor/indolence') meaning 'do not be lax'—the negative imperative applies to the preceding quality. Word order is typical of classical Persian: noun(s) + verbal imperative/negative.
segments.s652-g472: گزافه is an adjective/noun 'vain/false/nonsense'; مپرداز is a negative imperative 'do not cast/throw/afford' (here 'do not utter/do not squander'); مغزسخن is a compound 'the pith/kernel of speech' (object of مپرداز). Together: 'Do not utter vain things that waste the kernel of speech.'
segments.s652-g361: "به گیتی" is a locative prepositional phrase meaning "in/throughout the world"; here "به" introduces location. "همه تخم زفتی مکار" is a noun phrase in which "همه" quantifies "تخم" ("all the seed/offspring/brood") and the two following adjectives/epithets "زفتی مکار" stack after the noun in typical poetic order to mean "vile/dirty and deceitful."
segments.s652-g362: This clause gives the main comment: "ستیزه نه خوب آید" is a simple predication meaning "quarrels/strife is not good" or "quarrels do not suit/fit." "نه" is the negation placed before the verb phrase (classical/poetic order). "آید" is the present/future/habitual form "comes/is fitting," and "از شهریار" is an ablative/agent phrase "from/of a king" meaning "for a king" or "coming from a king" (i.e. "does not become a king").
segments.s652-g363: "گر" introduces a conditional clause ("if"). "از رای من" is an ablative/contrastive phrase "from my counsel / contrary to my advice." The sequence "سر به یک‌سو بری" uses the idiom "سر به یک سو بردن/بردنِ سر" (to turn/turn away one's head); here "سر" is the body noun "head," "به یک‌سو" indicates direction "to one side," and "بری" is an older/poetic verb form functioning as the verb of the clause (hypothetical/subjunctive/second-person sense: "you turn/you should turn/you go aside").
segments.s652-g364: "بلندی گزینی" is a verb phrase in which "گزینی" (from گزیدن) means "you will choose/you choose" and "بلندی" ("height/loftiness, i.e. high status") is its object, so together "you will choose loftiness/ high rank." The conjunction "و" links a second coordinate item "کنداوری," here a proper name/place treated as another object or outcome: "and Kandaavari." The clause thus predicts the choice of exaltation and the (person/place) Kandaavari.
segments.s652-g365: "بسی پند پیروز یاد آیدت" is a clause in which "بسی پند" means "many/much advice" ("بسی" quantifies "پند"). "پیروز" here qualifies or names the source of those counsels (either as an epithet or a name). "یاد آیدت" is a fixed verbal phrase meaning "will come to your memory/you will recall/you will think of" (literally "memory comes to you").
segments.s652-g366: This second clause repeats the verb phrase "یاد آیدت" with a different subject: "سخن‌های بد گوی" — here "سخن‌های" (plural "words/sayings") modified by the compound "بد گوی" ("ill-speaking" or "slanderous"); syntactically "بد گوی" functions as an adjective/epithet. So the clause says "ill-speaking words will come to (your) memory/you will recall slanderous sayings."
segments.s652-g367: "چنین داد پاسخ ورانوش‌زاد" is verb-final poetic order where "چنین" ("thus/so") modifies the verb "داد" (past tense "gave"), "پاسخ" is the direct object "an answer/reply," and "ورانوش‌زاد" is the subject (proper name). Literally: "Thus gave answer Varanushzad," i.e. "Varanushzad answered thus."
segments.s652-g368: "که‌ای پیر فرتوت سر پر ز باد" is a vocative/exclamatory address beginning with "که‌ای" (classical/poetic vocative like "O"). "پیر فرتوت" is a compound epithet "old, decrepit one;" "سر پر ز باد" is a metaphorical predicative phrase literally "a head full of wind" meaning "empty-headed/foolish." Word order places the epithets before the predication in typical poetic style.
segments.s652-g345: This clause is a negative copular statement: subject 'تو' (you) + prepositional comparison 'با شاه کسری' (with King Khosrow) + predicate 'بسنده' (sufficient). The verb 'نه‌ای' is an archaic negative form of the copula (lit. 'are not'); word order is emphatic Persian (topic first: 'تو با شاه کسری' then predicate).
segments.s652-g346: Introduced by 'وگر' = 'and if / even if', this conditional repeats the negative copula 'نه‌ای' but omits an explicit adjective; 'پیل و شیر دمنده' ('elephant and a lion that breathes/roars') is a nominal phrase functioning as the hypothetical predicate. The clause relies on ellipsis: the sense of 'بسنده' (suffice) from the previous clause is understood and not repeated.
segments.s652-g347: 'چو' here is temporal ('when'); the linked nouns 'دست و عنان' ('hand and reins') name the instruments of rule and take the predicate 'توای شهریار'—an older adjectival/nominal form meaning 'are yours / belong to you' or 'you are able/fit, O king'. Word order places the time/condition clause first.
segments.s652-g348: This short clause contains an archaic/obscure item 'بایوان' (marked in the glosses as uncertain/archaising) followed by 'شاهان' (kings) and the past negative verb 'ندیدم' ('I have not seen'). The final 'نگار' is a vocative ('O beloved' or 'O fair one'), so the whole means roughly 'I have seen no (peer) among kings, O beloved.' Note the verb 'ندیدم' is past with the 1st-person suffix.
segments.s652-g349: Again 'چو' = 'when/as'; three coordinated nominal elements ('پای' — hoof/step, 'رکیب' — companion or rider here, 'یال' — mane) are listed with possessive 'تو' after each to emphasize ownership. Persian allows repeated pronouns for poetic emphasis; the sequence is a descriptive nominal clause setting up the following comment.
segments.s652-g350: 'چنین' ('such') qualifies the ensuing nouns. 'شورش' here means 'commotion/tumult'; the construction 'چنین شورش و دست و کوپال تو' strings together more qualities — 'دست' = power/hand, and 'کوپال' is a proper name (a named weapon or epithet). The verb is again omitted; the clause is descriptive and relies on earlier verbal context to complete the sense.
segments.s652-g351: Literal SOV order: agent 'نگارنده' (the painter/artist) + object 'چین نگاری' ('the making/modeling of folds/features') + verb 'ندید' (did not see). The negation is on the past verb ('ندید' = did not see). The phrase means 'no artist has seen/painted such feature-work.'
segments.s652-g352: Here 'زمانه' (the age/world) is the grammatical subject and 'چو تو' ('like you') is a simile introduced by 'چو' = 'like'; 'شهریاری ندید' ('has not seen a kingship/sovereignty') is the negated past clause. Together: 'the age has not seen a sovereignty like yours.'
segments.s652-g449: The phrase places the destination before the source: 'به تابوتش' (to/into his coffin) marks the destination, followed by 'از جای برداشتند' (they took/removed from the place). 'برداشتند' is past tense, 3rd-person plural, meaning 'they lifted/removed'; the possessive enclitic -ش on تابوتش = 'his coffin'. The object (the body/coffin) is implied in the verb.
segments.s652-g450: 'سه فرسنگ' gives the distance (three parasangs) and functions adverbially. 'بر دست بگذاشتند' literally 'they placed [it] upon (their) hands' — an idiom for carrying something on the hands/shoulders; 'بگذاشتند' is past, 3rd-person plural. The direct object (the coffin) remains understood rather than repeated.
segments.s652-g451: 'چوآگاه شد' = 'when he became aware / when he was informed' (چو = when; شد = became/was). 'زان سخن مادرش' = 'of that speech/word of his mother' — زان = 'from that', سخن = 'speech/word', مادرش = 'his mother' (possessive -ش). The clause is a temporal subordinate: when X happened, Y followed.
segments.s652-g452: 'به خاک' = 'to/onto the earth' (prepositional phrase of direction). 'اندرآمد' here is used as a verb meaning 'fell down' / 'came down' / 'was thrown down' (classical usage), and it takes the coordinated object 'سر و افسرش' ('his head and his helmet'). Word order is somewhat inverted (destination before verb). Note: the provided dictionary senses for اندرآمد do not include this verbal sense, so that entry is not matched below.
segments.s652-g453: 'ز پرده' = 'from (behind) the curtain' (ز = from). 'برهنه' = 'uncovered / unveiled' (here adjectival, describing how she came out). 'بیامد' is past tense 'came'. 'به راه' is the destination 'into the road/street'. Word order places the originating point and manner before the verb and then the destination.
segments.s652-g454: This clause reports the state of the place: 'انجمن گشته بازارگاه' = 'the marketplace had become an assembly/gathering' (گشته = perfect/participial 'has become'). 'برو' here appears with narrative force linked to the preceding movement (literally 'go' / 'went'), so the clause as a whole reads as a short, poetic report: she came out, for the marketplace had turned into an assembly. Grammatically, 'انجمن' is predicate ('an assembly') and 'گشته' is the participial verb 'become'; 'بازارگاه' is the locative/subject.
segments.s652-g455: 'سراپرده‌ای' refers to the inner apartment/inner curtain (an element of the private chamber). 'گردش' is 'a turning / a circuit / a movement', and 'اندر زدند' is the past plural verb 'they struck / they made (a movement or sound) within' (زدند with اندر 'within'). Together the phrase describes activity originating from the inner chamber: a movement/round was made or performed within. The verb is past 3rd-person plural.
segments.s652-g456: 'جهانی همه' = 'all the world / everyone' (جهانی qualifying همه). 'خاک بر سر زدند' is an idiomatic mourning action: 'they beat/put dust on their heads' — 'خاک' (dust), 'بر' (on/upon), 'سر' (head), and 'زدند' (they struck/dashed). Word order is subject ('everyone') + manner/result clause 'they struck dust on (their) heads', with زدند in past plural.
segments.s652-g385: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/commander'. چوآتش here is a temporal subordinating phrase 'چو آتش' = 'when (he) kindled the fire' and برانگیخت is a past-tense verb 'roused/kindled' (literary/past), so the whole chunk is a temporal clause 'when the commander kindled the fire'. اسب (horse/steed) appears after that clause as the following main NP (the thing that comes).
segments.s652-g386: بیامد is a simple past 'came'. بکردار is an attributive adjective 'of noble/well‑behaved bearing' placed before the following name; آذر گشسب is a proper name (here the name/epithet of the steed). The sequence is verb → adjective → name and reads 'a steed came — bəkdār Azar‑Gushasp'.
segments.s652-g387: چپ لشکر شاه ایران is a genitive/possessive chain 'the left (wing) of the army of the king of Iran' (noun cluster with modifiers before the verb). ببرد is a literary 3rd‑person past/present form of برد meaning 'took/advanced' (here past sense 'advanced/moved'); word order places the verb at the end after the noun phrase.
segments.s652-g388: به پیش سپه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/in front of the host' (به + پیش + سپه). در نماند is the negative past 'did not remain/there remained none' (در = 'there/in that place', نماند = 'remained'). ایچ is the emphatic negative 'nothing/not a single one', and گرد functions here as part of the negating/encompassing expression ('around/at all'), so the whole clause means 'toward the front of the host there remained not a single thing/man.'
segments.s652-g389: فراوان is an adverb/quantifier 'abundantly/many'. ز مردان لشکر is a partitive/ablative phrase 'from among the men of the army'. بکشت is a literary past verb meaning 'he slew' (archaic form with the verbal prefix بـ). Together: 'he slew many of the army's men.'
segments.s652-g390: ازان کار is an adverbial phrase 'from that deed/thereupon' (shows cause or consequence). شد is the past of شدن 'became'. رام is a predicate adjective 'tame/ subdued' placed before the personal name that follows; برزین درشت is a compound personal name/epithet. The clause reads 'thereupon/ because of that deed, Barzin‑Deresht became tame.'
segments.s652-g391: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that', and تیرباران کنند is a verbal phrase 'they make a shower of arrows / they rain arrows' where کنند is the plural present/subjunctive used for reported/commanded actions. The whole segment = 'he ordered that they rain arrows.'
segments.s652-g392: هوا is the subject 'the air/the sky'. چون tگرگ is a simile 'like hail'. بهاران here means 'of spring' (time/season) and together with کنند (they/make) continues the result clause: 'they made the air like hail in spring' — i.e. arrows flew so densely that the air resembled a spring hailstorm.
segments.s652-g369: This is a negative imperative clause. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'; لشکر = 'army/host'; the sequence م + را represents the enclitic possession/object marking (i.e. لشکرم را ‘my army’ as the object). زینهاری 'warning/entreaty' is the direct object, and مخواه is the negative imperative 'do not seek/ask for'. Word order is (from) + (army + poss./obj) + (object) + (imperative).
segments.s652-g370: سرافراز is predicate/adjective 'honored, triumphant' describing the person to be made; گردان here functions as an imperative (from گرداندن/گردانیدن) 'cause to be / make' (poetic usage). و connects to the following noun phrase فرزند شاه 'a son of the king' (object/complement describing the desired state).
segments.s652-g371: This clause is impersonal/psychological: مرا here marks the experiencer/beneficiary ('to me' / 'for me'), دین کسری = 'Khosrow’s religion/faith' (possessive relationship), and نباید is an impersonal negative 'it is not proper/necessary' or 'I do not need / I ought not'. همی is an archaic particle giving durative/emphatic force.
segments.s652-g372: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م), سوی مادر is a prepositional phrase 'toward (his) mother', and گراید is a present-tense/gnomic verb 'turns/leans' (گراییدن = to incline). همی again adds continuous/emphatic aspect: 'keeps turning / still turns.'
segments.s652-g373: This is a subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that/for'. دین مسیحا است is copular: 'the religion is (that) of the Messiah' with the fused poetic form مسیحاست (مسیحا + است). آیین اوی is an appositive/clarification 'his ritual/custom' with اوی as the 3rd-person possessive.
segments.s652-g374: نگردم is a negative present/subjunctive 'I will not turn/be turned (away)' (ن + گرَدم), من is emphatic subject, از introduces the source 'from', فره = 'divine glory/splendour', و links the two objects, and دین اوی = 'his religion' (possession marked by اوی). The clause means 'I will not be turned away from his glory and his faith.'
segments.s652-g375: مسیحای دین‌دار is a noun phrase 'the pious Messiah' (مسیحا with the adjectival/determinative -ی and دین‌دار 'religious/pious'). اگر + کشته شد is a protasis 'if was killed' — کشته شد is the passive past 'was killed' (کشته + شد acting as auxiliary/passive marker).
segments.s652-g376: This is the consequent/contrast: نه is negative 'not/it is not so', فر 'farr' (divine royal glory) is the theme, جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign' is appositive, ازو 'from him' gives the source, and گشته شد is a passive/perfect construction 'was made/was taken' (گشته = participle; شد auxiliary). Together: 'it did not happen that the sovereign's farr was taken from him.'
segments.s652-g305: This is a compact noun phrase placing titles/figures before a location: 'سپهدار' (spahbed/commander) followed by 'شماس' (shamas, an attendant/title). 'پیش اندرون' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before / in front of the inner (apartments)'; 'پیش' here functions as 'before/in the presence of' and precedes the locative 'اندرون'. The sequence is typical of Persian noun phrases where modifiers and locative complements follow the head.
segments.s652-g306: An independent clause describing the army: 'سپاهی همه' = 'the soldiers/all the army'. 'دست شسته' is a participial phrase literally 'hands washed' modifying the army; in Persian the adjective/participle follows the noun. 'به خون' is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in/with blood' (here rendered 'washed in blood'), with 'به' linking the participle to its complement.
segments.s652-g307: 'برآمد' is the simple past ('arose/issued forth') and takes the noun 'خروش' as its subject/object: 'a cry/roar arose'. 'از در نوش‌زاد' is a prepositional phrase 'from the door/gate of Nushzad' (در = 'door, gate' here) introduced by 'از' meaning 'from/out of'. 'نوش‌زاد' is an epithet/compound literally meaning 'new-born' but here functions as a proper epithet/name after 'در'.
segments.s652-g308: 'بجنبید' is a past/plural or jussive/epic-form verb meaning 'they stirred/moved/ were roused'. The clause 'لشکر چو دریا ز باد' is a simile: 'the host like the sea from/in the wind' — 'چو' = 'like/as', 'دریا' = 'sea', and the preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') marks source/agent ('from/by the wind'). Word order is standard: verb then subject then simile phrase.
segments.s652-g309: 'به هامون کشیدند' is a verbal clause: 'کشیدند' (they drew/moved) with destination introduced by 'به هامون' ('to the plain'). 'یکسر' modifies the verb and here means 'all at once / straightaway' (emphatic motion). The trailing 'ز شهر' marks origin: 'from the city'. Word order: destination first (poetic/emphatic), verb, adverbial modifier, then origin.
segments.s652-g310: This is a string of adjectival/nominal phrases describing the place and the fighters: 'پر از جنگ' = 'full of war' (پر + از + noun is the standard 'full of X' construction). 'سر دل' is a compact phrase where 'سر' (here 'secret/inner thought' or 'mind') is linked to 'دل' (heart); the following 'پر از کین و زهر' repeats the 'پر از' pattern: 'full of hatred and venom/poison'. The structure stacks descriptive phrases without an explicit verb (common in epic poetry).
segments.s652-g311: 'چو' introduces a temporal/subordinate clause here meaning 'when'. 'گرد سپه' is a poetic expression for the army's host — literally 'the dust/cloud of the army' — with 'گرد' used image‑wise. 'رام برزین' appears as a proper name or epithet (in the line it functions as the person seen). 'بدید' is the past tense 'saw'. The phrase order is: temporal introducer + subject-group + verb.
segments.s652-g312: 'بزد' is the simple past 'he struck/blew' and takes 'نای رویین' ('iron ney' — an iron reed or trumpet) as its object: the instrument is postpositive. The second small clause 'وصف بر کشید' uses the light verb 'کشید' (past of کشیدن) with the prepositional particle 'بر' to mean 'raised/drew up' (here 'raised a cry/epithet/announcement'); such 'بر + کشیدن' combinations are frequent in Persian to mean 'lift/raise/utter'. The sequence shows a rapid pair of war actions.
segments.s652-g425: ازان رزمگه (= azān razmgah) is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from that battlefield' (azān = 'from that', رزمگه = 'battlefield'). کس نکشتند is an inverted clause: کس here functions as the (indefinite) object 'anyone/any person' and نکشتند is the past negative 'they did not kill' (ن + کشتند). نیز at the end attaches as 'also/either' — the whole segment: 'from that battlefield they did not kill anyone either.'
segments.s652-g426: نبودند شاد: نبودند is the past negated form of بودن, 'they were not' (here plural past 'were not'), modifying شاد 'happy/merry'. و introduces the next clause. نبردند چیز here uses نبردند (past of بردن/نبردن) with چیز 'thing' — literally 'they did not fight anything' or 'there was no fighting'; grammatically نبردند is a past verbal predicate and چیز is its object.
segments.s652-g427: و را کشته دیدند: را is the definite/object marker referring back to the person previously mentioned; کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and دیدند is 'they saw'. The sequence 'کشته دیدند' = 'they saw (him) slain.' و افگنده خوار continues with two adjectives/participles: افگنده 'thrown/cast down' (participle) and خوار 'despised/worthless', describing his state.
segments.s652-g428: سکوبای رومی is a noun phrase (a name or title qualified by رومی 'Roman/Byzantine'); سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic pronoun -ش). بر is the preposition 'on/upon/at' and کنار 'side' — together 'his head at the side' (i.e. his head lay at his side). Grammatically سکوبای رومی functions as the possessor/identifier of the head phrase.
segments.s652-g429: همه رزمگه گشت زو پر خروش: همه رزمگه 'the entire battlefield' is the subject; گشت is past 'became' (became/was turned). ز (poetic contraction of از) + و (here = او → 'him') = 'because of him/from him', and پر خروش 'full of cry/uproar' is a predicative phrase. The clause thus means 'the whole battlefield became, because of him, full of uproar.'
segments.s652-g430: دل رام برزین پر از درد و جوش: دل = 'heart'; رام برزین here is a personal name or name phrase (Ram‑Barzīn) in genitive relation to دل ('the heart of Ram‑Barzīn'). پر از = 'full of', درد 'pain/sorrow', و جوش 'and ardor/passion' — the sequence describes his inner state: 'his heart was full of sorrow and ardor.'
segments.s652-g431: زاسقف بپرسید: زاسقف = 'from the bishop' / 'of the bishop' (zā‑asqof, a poetic prepositional phrase), بپرسید is past 'he asked' (third person past). کزنوش زاد is a small clause: کز = 'who/which from', نوش 'drink/pleasure' (here 'of drink' or 'of wine'), and زاد is related to 'birth/origin' ('born of') — together 'who was born of drink' or 'which (one) is born of drinking'.
segments.s652-g432: از اندرز شاهی چه داری به یاد: از = 'of/regarding', اندرز شاهی = 'the king's counsel' (اندرز 'advice/counsel' + شاهی 'royal/of the king'), چه = 'what', داری = 'do you have/possess' (here 'do you have (in memory)'), به یاد = 'in memory/remembered'. The phrase is a direct question: 'What of the king's counsel do you recall?'
segments.s652-g393: This is a compact past‑narrative clause. بگرد is an archaic poetic past of گردیدن ('turn/return') used here as 'turned'; اندرون is a locative adverb 'inward/within'; خسته شد is a two‑word predicate 'became weary' (شد = become). نوش‌زاد appears after the verb phrase as the subject/name (poetic word order: verb before subject).
segments.s652-g394: بسی here functions as an adverb 'much/often'; کرد is the past verb 'did/made' but combines with یاد to form یاد کردن ('to remember'); از پند پیروز is a genitive/source phrase 'from Pīrūz’s advice'. The whole segment therefore means 'he often remembered Pīrūz's advice.'
segments.s652-g395: بیامد is simple past 'came'; به قلب is the prepositional phrase 'into the heart'; سپه (here 'host/multitude') is the subject, followed by پر ز درد 'full of/filled with pain' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'from/of'). Word order is poetic: verb + locative + nominal phrase describing what arrived.
segments.s652-g396: This gives two parallel descriptive predicates. تن 'the body' + از تیر خسته = 'weary from arrows' (از marks cause), and رخ 'the face' + از درد زرد = 'pale/wan from pain' (زرد = 'pale/wan'). خسته and زرد are predicative adjectives applied after the causal phrase.
segments.s652-g397: چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت 'he said' (past). پیش + دلیران روم = 'in the presence of the brave of Rūm' (پیش = 'before/in front of'); note the noun phrase places the addressees after the verb in typical epic word order.
segments.s652-g398: که introduces the reported content clause. جنگ پدر = 'the father's battle/fight' (genitive). زار and خوارست are predicative adjectives 'pitiable' and 'degrading' (خوارست here functions as 'is humiliating'); the final شوم is a 1st‑person optative/subjunctive ('may I become' / 'I may be') or adjectival 'ill‑omened' depending on reading; syntactically it closes the reported complaint about the situation.
segments.s652-g399: بنالید is past 'he moaned/wailed'; و links the clauses; گریان is a participial/adverbial phrase 'weeping'; سقف را is a marked object/topic 'to the roof/ceiling' (را is the direct‑object/marker); بخواند here means 'called/uttered/shouted' (بخواند used for 'call out' or 'address aloud').
segments.s652-g400: سخن هرچ بودش = 'whatever speech/words there were' (هرچ = 'whatever', بودش = 'there was/it was' with enclitic). به دل در براند = 'he brought/placed into the heart' (به دل = 'to the heart', در = 'in', براند from راند/ران 'to drive/put' used here as 'took to heart/kept in his heart'). The clause means he kept whatever was said in his heart.
segments.s652-g337: تو (subject) begins the clause: classical second‑person singular 'you'. با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him' marking the adversary. کنون is an adverb 'now' placed before the verbal phrase. جنگ سازی is a causative/nominalized verbal phrase 'make war / wage war' where سازی (from ساختن/ساخت) functions as the verb 'you make/prepare'; همی is the archaic continuous/habitual marker attached to the verbal phrase (classical Persian uses همی to mark ongoing or habitual action). The clause is thus 'You now, with him, make war (you are making war).'
segments.s652-g338: سرت literally 'your head' (the possessor is the subject 'you'); به آسمان is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward/up to the sky'. برفرازی is the verbal/adjectival element meaning 'you raise aloft' (built on the root فراز/فرازیدن); here it functions as the predicate 'you raise ... aloft'. همی again marks the continuous/habitual aspect. Word order is typical classical Persian: possessor/affected noun (سرت) + direction + verbal predicate + همی.
segments.s652-g339: بدین here is the fused locative/demonstrative 'به + این' ('with/by this'), introducing the description. چهرچون = چهره چون, 'a countenance like' (چون = 'as/like'); ماه = 'the moon' (a praise for beauty). و این فرو برز links a second feature: این 'this/that', فرو 'down/low' (prefix) + برز 'high/exalted' — a poetic oxymoron describing a feature 'low‑set yet proud/exalted' (classical Persian often compresses compounds). The whole segment lists features of the person/thing being described.
segments.s652-g340: برین is بر + این ('on/upon this/that'), a prepositional demonstrative used before nouns; it occurs twice in parallel. یال و کتف = 'mane and shoulder' (coordinated nouns), and برین دست و گرز = 'upon that hand and mace'. The construction lists parts ('یال و کتف' then 'دست و گرز') each governed by the preceding demonstrative برین; coordination is simple and typical for descriptive catalogues in epic verse.
segments.s652-g341: نبینم is first‑person singular negative present 'I do not see' (classical form: ن + بینم). خرد 'wisdom' is the direct object; هیچ 'at all/no' negates it (intensifier placed after the noun is common in Persian). نزدیک تو is the locative 'near you' (نزدیک + pronoun). The clause therefore means 'I see no wisdom near you.'
segments.s652-g342: چنین 'thus/so' introduces the result clause. خیره شد is a two‑word verbal predicate: خیره (stunned/astonished) used predicatively and شد (past of شدن) 'became' — together 'became stunned/astounded'. جان تاریک تو 'your dark soul' is the affected subject phrase placed after the verb for emphasis (typical classical ordering). The clause describes the resulting state of the person's soul.
segments.s652-g343: دریغ is an interjection/noun 'pity/alas' introducing lamentation. آن سرو = 'that cypress' (a conventional image for tall beauty or noble lineage). تاج و نام و نژاد lists possessions/qualities: تاج 'crown' (sovereignty/honor), نام 'name/fame', نژاد 'lineage'. The phrase laments loss of rank and reputation.
segments.s652-g344: که is the relative 'which/that' linking the lament to what follows. اکنون 'now' sets time. همی‌داد خواهی is a classical periphrasis: همی marks continuous aspect on داد (to give), while خواهی (here 'you will') gives the future sense—together 'you will give away' or 'you will cast away'. به باد is the idiom 'to the wind' (به باد دادن = to waste or cast to the winds). The clause therefore means 'which you will now cast to the wind.'
segments.s652-g457: به خاکش سپردند is a past-tense transitive clause: به + خاکش (into/to his dust/earth) is the prepositional object and سپردند is 3rd-person-plural past 'they consigned/entrusted'. The following و شد نوش‌زاد joins with a simple past/inchoative شد 'became' (3rd person, implicit subject carried over) plus the predicate adjective نوش‌زاد 'newly born' — a poetic intransitive result clause.
segments.s652-g458: ز باد آمد is literal 'came from the wind' with ز as the poetic 'from' and آمد in simple past. The second half و ناگهان شد به باد uses ناگهان 'suddenly' and شد 'became/happened' plus the phrase به باد 'into/by the wind' (به here marks motion/tendency into). The sequence describes sudden disappearance or being carried off by the wind; شد is inchoative/passive-like.
segments.s652-g459: همه جند شاپور is a fronted subject phrase 'all the army of Shapur' (همه + noun), and گریان شدند is a past-tense predication: گریان functions as a predicative adjective/participle 'weeping' and شدند is 3rd-person-plural past 'they became' (so 'they began to weep' / 'they were in tears').
segments.s652-g460: ز درد دل شاه is an ablative/cause phrase 'from / because of the king’s heart-pain' (ز as 'because of' here, درد دل = emotional pain). بریان شدند is a result clause with بریان used figuratively 'consumed/scorched (by grief or passion)' and شدند 'they became' (3rd-plural). The whole line says they were consumed by sorrow caused by the king's pain.
segments.s652-g461: چه پیچی is an exclamatory phrase: چه (exclamative 'what/how') + پیچی (a noun meaning 'twist/trick/cunning'); همی is an archaic particle meaning 'still'/'continually' placed before the verbal/adjectival predicate; خیره is a predicative adjective 'staring/astounded'; در بند آز is a prepositional phrase 'in the bond/fetter of greed' (در = in, بند = fetter, آز = greed). The line addresses someone’s astonished state caused by greed.
segments.s652-g462: چودانی (classical spelling of چو دانی) is a conditional/introductory 'if/when you know' or 'as you know'; که is the relative/conjunction 'that'; ایدر 'here'. نمانی is the 2nd-person present/imperfect of ماندن with an initial ن that functions as negation in classical lines (contextually read as 'you will not remain'); دراز 'long' modifies the verb. Grammatically this reads like 'if/as you know, you will not remain here long.'
segments.s652-g463: گذرجوی is a vocative 'wayfarer' / 'passer-by' addressing the listener; the coordinating و links the admonition. چندین جهان را is the object phrase 'many worlds' marked by را (definite/object marker). مجوی is an archaic prohibitive/formal negative imperative 'do not seek' (م- prefix + juy-), so the whole clause is an exhortation: 'O traveler, do not seek many worlds.'
segments.s652-g464: گلش 'its flower/rose' (گل + possessive -ش), زهر دارد 'has poison' (زهر = poison, دارد = has). به سیری modifies the following verb as 'to/for satiety' (سیری = satiety), and مبوی is an archaic negative imperative from بوییدن 'to smell' (م- prefix forming prohibition) meaning 'do not smell.' Together: 'Its rose has poison — do not smell it to satiety / do not indulge in it.'
segments.s652-g329: مسیح فریبنده is the noun phrase subject (the Messiah described as 'deceitful'). خود here is reflexive/intensive ('himself') modifying the passive predicate. کُشته شد is a passive perfect/periphrasis (perfect participle کُشته + auxiliary شد) meaning 'was killed/was slain.' Word order is subject → (reflexive) → passive predicate.
segments.s652-g330: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از دین یزدان is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from the religion of God.' سرش (سر + ـش) 'his head' is the theme; گشته شد is again the participle گشته 'turned/been made' plus auxiliary شد, yielding 'was turned/was made to be,' i.e. 'was turned away.' The clause literally reads 'when from God's religion his head was turned,' i.e. 'when he turned away from God's religion.'
segments.s652-g331: ز دین آوران is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'toward/from the bringers of religion.' کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred' and stands before the verb (classical word order). آنکس مجوی: مجوی is a prohibitive/negative imperative (2nd person) 'do not seek/pursue'; آنکس ('that person') is its object. Together: 'Seek not vengeance against the religion-bringers; do not pursue that man.'
segments.s652-g332: کجا is an interrogative/adverb 'where' or 'in what respect' used rhetorically here. کارِ خود را is the definite object 'his own work/business' (کار + possessive + object marker را). ندانست is the simple past negative 'did not know.' روی here functions adverbially ('upon/with respect to' or 'to show/turn [the face]') — together the clause asks in what respect the man lacked knowledge of his duty.
segments.s652-g333: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' فر یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the divine radiance/glory of God.' برو تافتی is a compressed classical expression equivalent to بر او تافتی ('shone upon him'); تافتی (from تافتن) is the verb 'shone.' The clause therefore means 'if God's glory shone upon him.' Note: برو here is contraction of بر او (not the verb 'go').
segments.s652-g334: جهود here is the archaic/classical word for 'the Jews' (not 'efforts'), and اندرو = اندر + او / اندرِ آن 'in it/him' meaning 'into that.' راه is 'way/path.' کی یافتی is a rhetorical interrogative construction: کی (here 'how/when/ever', interrogative) + یافتی (past/subjunctive sense 'would have found') — 'how would (they) have found a way into it?'.
segments.s652-g335: پدرت is the noun پدر 'father' with the 2nd-person possessive suffix ـت 'your father.' آن جهاندار آزادمرد is appositive description: آن 'that/the,' جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign,' آزادمرد 'noble/generous man.' The phrase strings the possessor + honorific epithets in classical order.
segments.s652-g336: شنیدی is the past second-person 'you heard' or 'have you heard.' که introduces the reported/subordinate content. با روم و قیصر 'with (or against) Rome and the Caesar' names the parties affected. چه کرد is a colloquial classical clause 'what he did' (چه = what; کرد = did). Together the clause asks whether the addressee heard of the great deeds his father did to Rome and the Caesar.
segments.s652-g409: مرا بهره اینست is a compact, archaic way to say “my share/lot is this.” مرا here functions as the dative/accusative pronoun (“for me”/“my”), بهره means “share/lot,” and اینست (این + است) is the copula ‘this is’ placed after the noun for emphasis — a common poetic word order.
segments.s652-g410: دلم چون بدی شاد و گیتی‌فروز: دلم is ‘my heart’ (noun + enclitic), چون here means ‘when/as,’ and بدی is used as the state ‘badness/sorrow’ (i.e. ‘when my heart is afflicted’). شاد و گیتی‌فروز are coordinate predicates ‘happy/cheerful and world‑illuminating’; the copula is omitted (typical in poetry) and the adjectives describe the heart’s condition.
segments.s652-g411: نزاید is the poetic 3rd‑person verb of زادن ‘to be born/come into being’ (here used in a general statement), followed by جز از مرگ ‘except from death.’ را before جانور is the accusative/definiteness marker attached in front of the noun that follows; the clause says essentially that no creature is born except by/for death (i.e. mortality is universal).
segments.s652-g412: اگر مرگ دانی غم من مخور is conditional: اگر + clause ‘if you know death’ (دانی = 2nd person singular of دانستن ‘to know’), followed by an imperative/negative admonition غم من مخور ‘do not consume (i.e. do not be consumed by) my grief’ — colloquially ‘do not grieve for me.’ The negated verb مخور is literal ‘do not eat’ used idiomatically.
segments.s652-g413: سر من ز کشتن پر از دود نیست: noun + enclitic سر من ‘my head,’ then the source phrase ز کشتن ‘from killing/slaughter,’ followed by the predicate پر از دود ‘full of smoke,’ and the copula/negator نیست ‘is not.’ In poetic order the predicate (پر از دود) precedes the final negation; کشتن is a verbal noun ‘killing/slaughter.’
segments.s652-g414: پدر بتر از من که خشنود نیست: comparative construction where بتر (comparative ‘worse’) takes از من ‘than I.’ که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘who/for he’ (i.e. ‘because he’) and خشنود نیست ‘is not pleased/ content.’ The whole says the father is worse off than the speaker because he is not content.
segments.s652-g415: مکن is the negative imperative ‘do not.’ It governs a series of nouns linked by و: دخمه (tomb/crypt), تخت (bed/throne — here likely the bier or funeral bed), and رنج دراز ‘long sorrow/extended mourning’ (دراز postmodifies رنج). The speaker forbids preparing these funeral trappings.
segments.s652-g416: به رسم مسیحا ‘in the custom/fashion of the Messiah’ (i.e. in Christian fashion) introduces the manner; یکی گور ساز is an imperative (inverted word order) telling the addressee to make/prepare one grave. ساز here functions as a verb ‘make/construct’ in poetic usage and یکی (one) modifies گور (grave).
segments.s652-g273: مباش is a prohibitive imperative ('do not be'). اندرین is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/among these' that follows the verb in this poetic order. نیزهمداستان (a compound poetic form: نیز هم‌داستان) is a predicate/adjectival phrase 'also in agreement / of the same mind' describing the state the speaker forbids.
segments.s652-g274: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that/who'. بدخواه is the noun subject 'ill-wisher'. راند is the simple lexical verb (classical form of راندن) here meaning 'drives / spreads / puts about' and functions as the verb of the clause. چنین داستان is the object phrase 'such a tale / such a story' (چنین 'thus/such' modifying داستان), completing the clause.
segments.s652-g275: گراو here functions as a classical/poetic verbal/adjectival form meaning 'if/if he is turned / if he is inclined' (poetic contraction). بی + هنرشد is a compound meaning 'became without skill / was made lacking in art' (بی = without, هنرشد = 'was made/was in relation to art/skill'); هم 'too/also' links him to what was said before. ازپشت ماست is a causal/possessive phrase 'is from our back' idiomatically 'is our fault' with ست (copula) supplied in the next word group.
segments.s652-g276: دل ما is the subject 'our heart'. برین is the fused/poetic form of بر + این ('on this') and introduces the phrase 'on/for this truth'. راستی is the noun 'truth' or 'this rightness'. برگواست is the predicate: a (poetic/irregular) copular/nominal phrase asserting that the heart serves as witness/proof to that truth (here used as 'is witness/attests').
