segments.s652-g277: زبان is used here as 'speech / utterance' (the act of speaking). کسی کو is a classical relative phrase 'someone who / of whom' (کو functioning in older usage to link the relative idea). ببد is an archaic/poetic perfective/constative 'turned / became (bad)' describing the tongue; کرد is the light verb 'did/caused' taking یاد ('memory/remembrance') as its object: 'caused remembrance / made remembered.' The whole clause says roughly 'the speech of anyone whose tongue turned bad caused it to be remembered.'
segments.s652-g278: وزو (classical fused form, here 'from him' in context) + بود (past 'was') introduces a result/clause: 'and from him was ...'. بیداد here is used as a proper name (Bīdad) or as 'injustice' depending on reading; followed by برنوش زاد 'born of Barnūsh' (برنوش as a matronymic/proper name and زاد 'was born'). The clause therefore records a birth/origin: 'from him Bīdad, son/daughter of Barnūsh, was born.'
segments.s652-g279: همه is an imperative addressee 'all / everyone'. داغ کن is an imperative compound (داغ + کن) meaning 'make (a) mourning / cause a mark of grief' or 'brand/mark' depending on register; here it functions as an exhortation. برسر انجمن is a locative phrase 'upon/at the assembly' specifying where this action should take place; the verb (کن) governs that phrase in this elliptic poetic order.
segments.s652-g280: مبادش is an optative/negative wish 'may it not be' (a classical formula). زبان and دهن are the nouns 'tongue' and 'mouth' (here meaning 'speech' and 'utterance' or literally the organs), and the repeated مبادش with و (and) gives the sense 'may it not be his tongue, and may it not be his mouth' — i.e. a wish that the offender have no tongue/mouth or that they be silenced.
segments.s652-g401: بدو گفت is a fronted indirect object + past verb: بدو (to him) + گفت (he said). کین روزگارم دژم is a noun phrase apposition describing the speaker's lot: کین (hateful/grudging, here 'hateful') modifies روزگارم (my fate), and دژم is an adjective 'grim/sullen' placed after the noun as in older poetic Persian.
segments.s652-g402: This clause states the effect using آورد (past of آوردن) as the main verb; poetic preposition ز (from/of) introduces the source phrase and is followed by من (me). بر here has the locative/against sense 'upon', and چندین ستم is the affected noun phrase 'many injustices' placed after the verb (poetic word order: verb before object). The whole reads ‘(this fate) has caused/brought many injustices upon me.’
segments.s652-g403: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; چون functions as the conjunction 'when'. به خاک اندر آید سرم is a poetic way to say 'my head comes into the earth' — i.e. when I fall to the ground / am laid in the dust (a euphemism for dying); note the verb آید (comes) appears before the possessed noun سرم (my head) in this word order.
segments.s652-g404: سواری is 'a rider/horseman' (here the thing to be placed). برافگن is an archaic/poetic infinitival/imprecise jussive form meaning roughly 'to set up/raise/put' (poetic imperative sense). بر مادرم is 'over my mother' (بر = upon, مادرم = my mother, with -م possessive). The clause is therefore an instruction: place/raise a rider over my mother.
segments.s652-g405: بگویش is the imperative/command 'tell her/him' (بگو + enclitic ش). که introduces the content clause. شد + زین جهان here means 'has become/from this world' i.e. 'has departed this world' — classical Persian often uses شد with locative to indicate 'it happened/was done.' نوش‌زاد is used as the noun 'newborn' (possibly a name or epithet) and is the theme of the clause.
segments.s652-g406: سرآمدبدو is a fused poetic verbal phrase meaning 'his end came' / 'it befell him' (literally 'the end came to him'). روز governs the following coordinated nouns بیداد و داد 'oppression and justice' — together 'the day (period) of oppression and of justice' — and the whole phrase means that his time marked by both oppression and justice has ended.
segments.s652-g407: تو از من مگر دل نداری به رنج is a rhetorical question: تو (you) + از من (toward me / concerning me) + مگر (here rhetorical 'surely / don't you?') + دل (heart/compassion) + نداری (you do not have / you lack). به رنج 'in (my) suffering' specifies the circumstance. Overall: 'Do you not have any compassion for me in my hardship?'.
segments.s652-g408: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because.' اینست is 'this is,' رسم 'the custom/practice,' and سرای سپنج is a noun phrase 'the house/household of سپنج'; سرای here functions as a noun 'house/household' (poetic spelling/formation), and سپنج is a figurative or obscure word (literally 'sponge') used as a household epithet. The clause says 'for this is the custom of the (so-called) house of سپنج.'
segments.s652-g353: This is a compressed nominal phrase + negative imperative: 'جوانی دل شاه کسری' is a genitive chain (javanī-e del-e shāh-e Kasrī in prose: 'the youth of the heart of King Khosrow') placed before the verb for poetic effect. 'مسوز' is a negative imperative (مَسوز = 'do not burn/consume/grieve'); the verb governs the whole preceding noun phrase.
segments.s652-g354: 'مکن' is the prohibitive auxiliary 'do not' and here introduces the second admonition. 'تیره' is an adjective 'dark/bleak' describing what should not be made; 'این آب گیتی‌فروز' is the direct object, with 'آب' used metaphorically as vital/essence (modified by the compound adjective 'گیتی‌فروز' = 'world‑illuminating'). The word order is inverted for poetic rhythm: literal sequence 'Do not make dark this world‑illuminating water.'
segments.s652-g355: 'پیاده شو' is the imperative 'dismount / become on‑foot'. The following short clause 'از باره زنهار خواه' is idiomatic/poetic: 'از باره' = 'in regard to this matter / for this occasion' and 'زنهار خواه' is an exhortation ('take heed / be warned') where خواه functions as a hortative/requestive element turning the noun 'زنهار' into a plea. The sequence is condensed and typical of Ferdowsi's poetic inversion.
segments.s652-g356: The verb phrase is elliptically placed: 'به خاک افگن' = 'cast/throw to the earth' where 'به خاک' marks the goal ('to the ground') and 'افگن' (here causative/forceful sense) is the verbal/adjectival element 'cause to fall / fling'. 'این گرز و رومی کلاه' lists the objects 'this mace and the Roman helmet' (קلاه = helmet). Word order places the object phrase after the verb phrase for meter and emphasis.
segments.s652-g357: Standard conditional opening: 'اگر' = 'if'. 'دور از ایدر' = 'from far away to here' (دور + از + ایدر), then 'یکی باد سرد' = 'a single/a certain cold wind' where 'یکی' is an indefinite article/quantifier and 'باد' is the noun 'wind'. Adjective 'سرد' follows the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s652-g358: This clause gives the result of the condition. 'نشاند' is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'set/placed' (here 'will set/settle'); 'بروی تو' = 'upon your face' (بِ + روی + تو, i.e. 'on your face'); 'بر تیره گرد' is a compact phrase meaning 'a dark (tint/gloom) [will be] upon/around' — 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon', 'تیره' the adjective 'dark', and 'گرد' here contributes the sense of 'turn/round' or 'a surrounding hue' in poetic usage. Altogether: 'it will settle upon your face a dark hue.'
segments.s652-g359: 'دل شهریار' is the subject 'the heart of the king'. 'از تو' gives the cause ('because of you'). 'بریان شود' is a stative/resultative construction: 'بریان' (literally 'roasted/burnt') is used figuratively 'consumed/burned (with passion or anger)', and 'شود' is the auxiliary 'will become / will be'. The clause means 'the king's heart will be burnt/consumed on your account.'
segments.s652-g360: 'ز روی تو' = 'because of your face' (ز = 'from/because of' + روی = 'face'). The predicate 'خورشید گریان شود' has inverted poetic order: 'خورشید' (the sun) is the logical subject and 'گریان شود' means 'will be made to weep' ('گریان' = 'weeping/causing to weep' and 'شود' = 'become'). The line hyperbolically says that even the sun would weep over your face.
segments.s652-g249: که begins a relative/clause-linking particle meaning 'that' or 'who.' پوشیده رویان او در نهان is a nominal phrase: پوشیده 'hidden' qualifies رویان (here 'the hidden ones' — an archaic/poetic plural noun), او is a possessive/agentive pronoun 'his,' and در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/secretly.' Word order is noun phrase + possessor + adverbial, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s652-g250: سرآرند is a third-person plural verbal form (poetic/classical) meaning 'they appear / they arise.' برخویشتن بر زمان is a compound adverbial: برخویشتن 'for themselves / at their own will' and بر زمان 'at the (right) time' — together 'at their own appointed time.' The verb agrees with the plural subject implied in the previous noun phrase.
segments.s652-g251: هم is an emphatic/adversative particle 'even'/'also.' ایوان او is 'his iwan/portico.' ساز here is a nominal use 'means/device' (i.e. 'became the means of'), and زندان اوی is a genitival phrase 'his prison' (اوی an archaic possessive). Classical Persian often omits an explicit copula, so the clause reads as 'even his iwan (became) the means of his prison' — idiomatically 'even his hall became his prison.'
segments.s652-g252: ابا is a verb meaning 'refused / were reluctant.' آنک is a conjunction 'that / because' introducing the causal clause. بردند is third-person plural past 'they took / seized,' and فرمان اوی is 'his command/authority' (اوی = his). The sequence gives a causal explanation: 'they refused, because they had seized his command.'
segments.s652-g253: در گنج is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the treasure / in the vault.' یک سر is a quantifying noun phrase 'one (head/one person / one chamber)' and بدو is the dative 'to him / to it.' برمبند is an archaic/poetic verbal form indicating confinement or fastening (here used as a past action 'they shut him up / they confined him'). Together the clause means 'they confined him in a treasury-vault.'
segments.s652-g254: وگرچه introduces a concessive clause 'although / even though.' چنین 'thus' modifies the following predicate. خوار شد is a light-verb construction where خوار (despised/humiliated) is the predicative adjective and شد 'became' is the past auxiliary; ارجمند (literally 'esteemed') here names the person/status that suffered—so the phrase means 'although thus the esteemed/noble one became despised/humiliated.' Note the inverted order for poetic emphasis (adjective + شد + noun).
segments.s652-g255: ز (poetic 'from') begins a list of sources/categories. پوشیده modifies رویان (here 'hidden things/hidden ones') and the phrase is parallel to the following از + خوردنی: از خوردنی 'from what is eaten / from edible things.' The construction enumerates different classes (z X and az Y).
segments.s652-g256: The pattern repeats with ز + a deverbal/nominal adjective: ز افگندنی 'from that which is cast/that which is thrown' and هم ز گستردنی 'and also from that which is spread/laid out.' افگندنی and گستردنی are nominalized verbal stems (adjectival nouns) used as nouns — a common poetic device to list kinds of things.
segments.s652-g257: برو is an imperative 'go' addressed to someone. The clause 'هیچ تنگی نباید به چیز' uses نباید impersonally ('there must not be/should not be') with هیچ تنگی ('no shortage/constraint') and به + چیز marking the domain ('about/with respect to anything'). Word order is somewhat archaic — the negated impersonal predicate (نباید) follows the noun phrase it governs.
segments.s652-g258: نباید که + subjunctive (نیابد) forms a conventional prohibitive/negative-optative clause 'it must not be that ...'. چیزی is indefinite 'anything'. نیابد here is a subjunctive/negative-form of یافتن ('to find') — i.e. 'should find' (used negatively). به + نیز gives an adverbial nuance 'there/also' or 'in it as well' (classical phrasing).
segments.s652-g259: وزین is an archaic adjective 'weighty/dignified' placed before the noun in elevated poetic diction; it qualifies مرزبانان ('border-guard/governors'). ایرانیان identifies whose guards they are ('of the Iranians'). No connective particle is written — a typical classical noun phrase.
segments.s652-g260: هران کس is an older form meaning 'each/every person'. The relative clause 'که بستند با او میان' reads literally 'who they bound with him among/between' — بستند is past tense 'they bound/tied', with با او ('with him') and میان ('among/between') giving the relational frame; the clause is left open and will be continued by the following line.
segments.s652-g261: چو + present/subjunctive-form (گردی) = 'when/if you become' (classical temporal clause). پیروز is predicate/adjective 'victorious'. مپیچان is a prohibitive/negative imperative ('do not twist/entangle') addressed to the addressee; its object is سخن ('words/speech') — 'do not confuse them with words' or 'do not waste time in talk'.
segments.s652-g262: میانشان 'among/between them' sets the locus. به خنجر uses به instrumentally ('by/with a dagger' — classical usage), and به دو نیم کن is an imperative construction: 'make into two halves' (نیم = half, کن = do!). The sequence gives the violent instruction: 'with a dagger, cut into two halves.'
segments.s652-g263: هران کس 'every person/anyone' begins a relative clause. که introduces the clause 'who ...'. او refers back to the mentioned sovereign ('that one/the king'). دشمن پادشاست is a predicate construction meaning 'is an enemy of the king' (پادشاست here refers to the king/monarchy).
segments.s652-g264: به کام = 'to the appetite/desire' (i.e. for the eating/appetite of), نهنگش 'his whale' (metaphorical voracious beast), سپاری is an infinitival/verb 'to entrust/give over', and رواست is the copular judgment 'it is fitting/permissible'. Together: 'It is fitting to give him over to the whale's maw.'
segments.s652-g433: چنین داد پاسخ is a verb–object inversion common in classical Persian: Literally ‘thus gave (an) answer’ — چنان(ی) = thus, داد = gave (past), پاسخ = answer. The following که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of the answer; جز مادرش = 'except his mother' (جز = except; مادر + ش = 'his mother').
segments.s652-g434: برهنه نباید که بیند برش: نباید که + present (بیند) expresses prohibition/necessity ('must not / should not ...'). برهنه is the predicate adjective 'naked'. بیند is the present/subjunctive form 'see'; the final برش is a poetic/archaic object form (ش = 'him') attached to بر (here performing the role of an object clitic) rather than the modern او را / اش form; no listed sense matches برش exactly, so it is treated as an enclitic pronoun.
segments.s652-g435: تن خویش = 'his own body' (تن = body; خوی + ش = a classical split of خویش, here functioning as reflexive/possessive 'own' + poss. clitic). چون دید = temporal clause 'when he saw' (چون = when). خسته به تیر = postpositive adjective phrase 'weary from arrows' (خسته = weary; به indicating cause/agent 'by/from arrows').
segments.s652-g436: ستودان نفرمود is elliptical: ستودان is the infinitive/nominal 'praise' and نفرمود is past negative of فرمودن ('did not say/command' or 'did not give'). The sense here is 'he did not pronounce praise' or 'he neither praised nor ordered (gifts)'. The following و مشک و عبیر lists the usual perfume/gift items (مشک, عبیر) that he did not give; the conjunctions و simply link them.
segments.s652-g437: A triple negative list using repeated نه to enumerate what is absent: افسر (helmet), دیبای رومی (Roman/Byzantine brocade — دیبا = splendid silk, رومی qualifying it), تخت (throne). These are direct objects enumerated in negative: 'no helmet, no brocade, no throne.'
segments.s652-g438: چو = 'when'; از بندگان دید = 'he saw (that) from/among the servants' (از = from/of; بندگان = servants/subjects; دید = saw). تاریک بخت is a nominal predicate in inverted order meaning 'fortune dark' → 'his luck/fate turned dark.' The clause links the sight of lowly company to the change of fortune.
segments.s652-g439: برسم مسیحا is a temporal/occasional phrase: برسم here (from رسیدن / رسیدن constructions) means 'on the occasion / at the coming', used with مسیحا 'the Messiah' (mسیحا can be vocative elsewhere but here names the event). کنون = 'now'; مادر + ش = 'his mother' (possessive clitic).
segments.s652-g440: کفن سازد uses سازد (present/subjunctive or historical-present of ساختن) 'makes/is making' with کفن 'shroud' as its object. The following و گور و هم چادرش lists additional items she makes: گور 'grave' and هم چادرش 'and also her veil' — چادرش is the possessive noun 'her (own) veil' (ش referring back to مادرش). Parallel nouns are coordinated without repeating the verb.
